Chapter 5 • Loss, Grief, and Bereavement 	155
when the notification is for someone in their own life, all the counseling skills one has
learned and practiced may not be as effective as one would expect. In a professional role,
counselors traditionally have become involved with clients after a death notification to
assist individuals in dealing with the grieving and healing process. In crisis intervention,
it is likely that a crisis counselor will intervene when the death of a loved one causes so
much stress to the surviving significant other that it affects the person’s normal daily
functioning and, in some cases, increases suicide lethality.
It is also possible in every crisis situation outlined in this text that a counselor may
be called to give a death notification or provide support for someone who is the primary
death notifier. As counselor roles and job opportunities in the community become more
diverse (e.g., in police departments, fire departments, military settings, religious set-
tings, chaplaincies, crisis centers, hospitals, and other agencies such as Red Cross, Green
Cross, and Hospice), the likelihood of participating in a death notification increases.
With the increase of counselors working with first responders during national crises
(e.g., hurricanes, September 11 terrorist attacks), counselors have been placed in the role
of giving death notifications or taking part in giving death notifications more frequently.
Even if a counselor intends to work in a private practice, it is possible they may
be asked to assist with a death notification or be consulted about how to tell another
family member about a death. No two death notifications are the same due to the actual
event, unique relationships, ecological determinants, and the developmental level of
and perception of the death by the survivors. However, the level of preparedness of the
crisis counselor in the notification process may affect the notification, how a survivor
responds during the crisis state, and whether a person seeks professional counseling
in the future.
Although no crisis counselor who provides a death notification can ever be
fully prepared for the response of the person receiving it, several components form
the foundation for an effective death notification. As outlined in the training protocol
for the Maryland State Police (Jackson-Cherry, 2009; Kanable, 2010; Page, 2008)
and adapted for the training of crisis counselors for this section, death notifications
should be provided in person and in pairs, in an appropriate time frame with accurate
information, in plain language, and with compassion. In preparation, complete Activity
5.5 and Think About It 5.4.
Take a few minutes and reflect upon a time when you
were notified of a death. Who died? Who provided the
death notification? What were the circumstances? How
was the death relayed to you? What were some of your
initial feelings or thoughts? What kind of influence did
the actual process of the death notification have on
you? What made the notification process helpful? What
could have made the death notification process better?
ACTIVITY 5.5
Think about your own personal losses and where you
are in the grief process. If you were called on to issue a
death notification or act as a support counselor in one,
could you be present for the bereaved and provide the
necessary support?

-- 184 of 545 --

156 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
In Person and in Pairs
Whenever possible, death notifications should be made in person, not by telephone.
The human presence during the notification of the death of a loved one is essential. The
presence of a crisis counselor can be crucial if the survivor has a reaction that requires
assessment of self-risk or the need for immediate medical attention. Crisis counselors can
assist clients during the initial shock, which may be devastating and demobilizing and
may interrupt intact normal thought processes. Counselors can also help the survivor
formulate a plan for informing others close to the deceased.
It is helpful to work in pairs in case questions need to be answered, multiple survi-
vors are present with various needs (age and developmental appropriateness), and as a
safety precaution for the notifying team members. If possible, when there are children in
the same room, take the specified adults into a private area to notify them of the death.
It would be helpful for the crisis worker to have information on hand to share with those
receiving the notification on the grieving process and developmental grieving of children.
Team members may be a combination of law enforcement officers, chaplains,
professional counselors, pastoral counselors, medical staff, clergy, school counselors,
case managers, other family members, or friends of the victim. For example, in homicide
or suicide cases, the team ordinarily would consist of law enforcement and possibly a
clergy member and/or a professional counselor. Many crisis response teams work in
collaboration with law enforcement agencies; in these situations, members of the crisis
team may be called on to be present at the notification. In a hospital setting, the team may
consist of a medical doctor, nurse, chaplain, professional counselor, and a social worker.
In a school setting, the team may consist of the school counselor, administrator, and
teachers. In the military, depending on the branch of service, the team would consist of a
Casualty Assistance Officer and possibly a chaplain. If the family is living on the military
installation, a professional from the family support center may also be involved in the
notification. Depending on the setting and circumstances of the death in each example,
outside professional counselors may be included to assist with any crisis situation or
death notification.
In our society, family members do not always live in the same community or even
in the same state as one another, which makes the process of the death notification being
done in person sometimes impossible. Depending on the setting, crisis counselors may
not be able to offer an in-person notification. In these circumstances, contact can be made
with a medical examiner, law enforcement department, religious clergy, or friend or
family member in the survivor’s home area to deliver the notification in person to the
next of kin. In other cases, these persons may be called to act as a physical support while
the notification is done by phone.
In Time and with Accurate Information
Timeliness of a death notification is essential so that an individual is informed in person
by the appropriate individual rather than by hearing it from a third party. At the same
time, it is imperative to first verify the accuracy of the information to be sure that correct
information regarding the correct person is being given to a survivor. This must be done in
a short amount of time, given the nature of the event. For example, in law enforcement cases
it is important that relatives (next of kin) be notified in a timely manner and with accurate
information so that they do not see or hear the news of the death on the television or radio.

-- 185 of 545 --

Chapter 5 • Loss, Grief, and Bereavement 	157
When a death occurs on campus or in a school, it is also important for those closest
to the deceased to be notified initially. In these instances, it would be preferable for
administrators, crisis counselors, and faculty to receive the information first to prioritize
who should be notified (e.g., team members, classmates, close friends) and in what manner
(e.g., small groups, team players, homerooms, listservs). This also allows for those giving the
notifications to be able to assess whether more intervention is needed for some individuals.
Prior to any event, agencies and school settings should designate a contact person or
alternate person who will be responsible for collecting and dispensing information. Even
if a team is formed to gather information, only one person should be designated as the
communicator so that differing accounts or information are not relayed. Inaccurate or
differing information from several sources could cause confusion, chaos, or panic.
Accuracy of the information does not necessarily mean a crisis counselor has to
have the full details, as this depends on the situation. For example, the sudden and tragic
death of one of our graduate students was reported by a student to faculty the morning
following the incident. Although essential to provide information in a timely manner,
it would have been premature to inform other students without receiving confirmation
of the student’s death from the family member or another firsthand source. At the same
time, it was not as crucial to have the details of the circumstances (e.g., drunk-driving
case) as it was to confirm and provide accurate information as to the identity of the stu-
dent so that we could formulate a response to the students. For other situations, more
detailed information may be needed at the time of the death notification.
It is essential to make an accurate notification. Mistaken death notifications may
cause undue trauma and, in some cases, have caused medical complications for the
person being informed. Before the notification, move quickly to gather information
by contacting family members for confirmation and to issue condolences. Be sure that
the victim’s identity has been confirmed. If administrators need to report the death via
electronic communications, be sure the entire name of the deceased has been verified
(first names alone are not sufficient) to prevent any further chaos or generate rumors.
For example, in the above situation, all the students could not be contacted individually,
although faculty advised students in their classes. The student listserv needed to be used.
When using a listserv to provide follow-up on associated events (e.g., services, memorial
services), the one person appointed to communicate information should provide frequent
updates and give first and last names in all correspondence. It should not be assumed
just because previous e-mails were sent out that all previous messages were read by the
receivers. Messages may seem redundant, but accuracy should be consistent.
Correspondences with university administration (Vice President for Academic
Affairs, President, Dean, Student Services) should be made to ensure university policies
are followed. Contact with the Campus Ministry and Counseling Center should be made,
and these services should be relayed and made available to students, faculty, and staff
in an appropriate time frame.
Evaluating the uniqueness of the situation is imperative in order to provide any
other interventions. In the university student case, the student was enrolled in a practicum
placement. Contact with the on-site supervisor was necessary not only for notification
but also to ensure a plan for the deceased student’s clients. Furthermore, the religious
beliefs of the family needed to be explored and conveyed to the students before their
attendance at the funeral service. In this example, flowers were prohibited, and there were
specific expectations due to religious beliefs during the service, including prohibiting

-- 186 of 545 --

158 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
any outward expressions of emotions that were believed to prevent the passing of the
deceased from this world to the next. In the end, students needed an extra debriefing
with faculty after the service to work through their unexpressed grief. Now, read Voices
from the Field 5.4, which discusses how mistakes can be made during a death notification.
Lisa R. Jackson-Cherry
In working with a police department counseling center
during an internship experience, I was asked to ride
along on my first death notification to provide added
support. A briefing was conducted with the team as
we traveled to a small rural town. When we reached
our destination, the team leader knocked on the front
door, which was opened by an older woman. After the
leader introduced us, he asked the woman if she was
Mrs. Smith. “Yes,” she replied. The leader continued,
“I’m sorry to be the one to inform you that there was a
car accident and your husband, Bill, was killed. He did
not survive his injuries.” She appeared visibly shocked
and started to sob uncontrollably. As we sat down to
continue to talk, another woman came running from
the adjacent yard. The leader turned to the woman and
said, “Would you be willing to stay with Mrs. Smith
until we can contact her family? Her husband, Bill, was
in a car accident and died.” This woman also began
shaking and sobbing and said, “This is my mother-in-
law. I’m also Mrs. Smith and Bill is my husband. My
husband, Bill, is her son.” At this time, we knew we had
the correct family but the next of kin we were inform-
ing was not the correct person.
In all our preparation, we did not go over the
unique issues that we may encounter in a small rural
town. In this case, most of the family members lived
on the same street, many sharing the same names. In
not asking for the complete name of the woman we
were notifying and not asking the relation of Bill to
her, we not only gave devastating news of her son’s
death (not her husband’s death) but then gave a less
than full and compassionate notification to the actual
wife of the deceased. We did follow through with
debriefing and put into practice necessary procedures
to ensure a mistake like this never happened again.
This is one reason why I believe counselors should be
prepared to assist with death notifications and why it
is imperative to include this information in the text.
I think of this family often and wonder about the impact
of this notification on them, as it still weighs heavy on
my mind over 20 years later.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 5.4
Learning from Mistakes
In Plain Language
It is important that the death notification be given to the survivor in a calm, direct,
and simple manner by the crisis counselor. Too much or vague information can cause
survivors to panic before getting the full, accurate information. A crisis counselor can
demonstrate care by stating the notification in clear and concise language. For example,
“I have some very bad news to tell you” or a similar statement allows a brief but crucial
moment for the survivor to prepare for the shock. The follow-up statement should also
be provided in plain language, such as “Your daughter, Jill, was in a car crash and she
was killed.” It is important to avoid vague statements such as Jill was “lost,” “hurt,” or
“passed away.” It is important to include a definitive conclusion of what occurred, such
as “ . . . and she was killed” or “ . . . there were no survivors” to prevent conveying a
message of false hope of survival. It is important to refer to the victim by name to provide
another level of verification and to personalize the deceased.
One of the most important gifts a crisis counselor can offer to a survivor is the gift of
the crisis counselor’s control and calmness during the death notification. Overemotional
crisis counselors can increase the potential for panic or chaos. Survivors often have many
questions, and some questions may be asked multiple times. As with a child’s experience

-- 187 of 545 --

Chapter 5 • Loss, Grief, and Bereavement 	159
with a death notification, survivors will often repeat the same question for verification of
facts. It is crucial to patiently answer questions about the circumstances regarding the death
that a crisis counselor may be privy to, such as the location of the deceased’s body, how
the deceased’s body will be released and transported to a funeral home, and whether an
autopsy will be performed. However, the sharing of this information should always take into
consideration the developmental stage and clinical assessment of how much can be handled
by the survivor at the time of the notification. Oftentimes, crisis counselors may not know
the answer to a question, and it is better for them to acknowledge this to survivors than to
give false information. It is important for crisis counselors to state that they will attempt to
find the answers to the questions they cannot answer. If there are questions counselors have
offered to explore, it is imperative that the counselors follow up directly with the survivor,
even if another agency or office will be contacting the survivor to provide the information.
With Compassion
Presence and compassion may be the only resources crisis counselors can provide during
the initial death notification process. It is important for them to accept the survivor’s
reactions and emotions to the death notification and also important to recognize their
own emotions associated with the notification. Although overemotional crisis counselors
can increase confusion and possible panic with survivors, it is better for a crisis counselor
to express appropriate emotion than to appear cold and unfeeling.
It is also important to relay to survivors that death is a personal event and that
due to the unique situation, they may feel various emotions, including sadness, anger,
frustration, relief, and guilt. Normalizing the vast array of emotions that survivors may
experience during the grief process is important. Print resources that reveal the grieving
process, reactions to grief, explaining death to a child (if appropriate), and referrals
for mental health counseling or a funeral home may be helpful and can be left with
the victim. Imposing personal religious beliefs is not helpful and could be harmful.
Statements such as “This was God’s will,” “She led a full life,” and “I understand what
you are going through” do not demonstrate compassion or regard for the person’s
unique grief experiences associated with loss.
It is essential to take time to provide information, support, and direction to the survivor.
Never simply provide a death notification and leave. It is important to offer information
about funeral planning, the normal grief process, and referrals for counseling. The bereaved
may not be ready for counseling at that moment but may need it in the near future.
In addition, it is not recommended that the crisis team provide the survivor with
the victim’s personal belongings during the death notification. Survivors often need
time before accepting the victim’s belongings, but eventually survivors will want to take
possession of them. Likewise, crisis counselors should never transport survivors (to the
hospital or home or to other agencies) unless law enforcement is a part of the notification
team. No one can ever truly predict the emotional reaction to the death of a loved one,
and the safety of the crisis counselor is always a priority.
Follow-Up
Crisis counselors should always leave their names and phone numbers with survivors, or
information on a referring counselor, and a follow-up contact with the survivor should
be scheduled for the next day, since most funeral arrangements must be made soon after

-- 188 of 545 --

160 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
the death. Some individuals, depending on their social support system,
may request assistance from team members for funeral arrangements.
If a crisis counselor is asked by the survivor to help with these plans,
it is imperative to assist the survivor based on the customs, traditions,
religious beliefs, and personal preferences of the family. Most survivors
are confused at the initial notification, and others may feel abandoned
after the notification. Many survivors will want clarifications or may
need more direction on necessary arrangements.
Following up is an important last step in completing the death
notification process and may be a crucial factor enabling a survivor to
reach out for continued mental health services. Often, counseling ser-
vices are necessary for clients to work through the extended or complicated grieving
process. A death notification event may be the first exposure a survivor has to a mental
health professional, so the interaction could encourage or discourage further connections
with crisis counselors. The members of the notification team should be clear with one
another on any follow-up assignments.
Debriefing with Team Members
Debriefing with team members should occur immediately after the death notification. This
debriefing time should be used to plan for any follow-up with the survivors and to review
how the death notification process was implemented and received. Processing what went
well and what could be improved upon will be helpful for future death notifications.
In addition, death notifications can be stressful and emotionally draining to the indi-
viduals involved in the notification process. Team members should share concerns with one
another. It is important to discuss feelings or thoughts associated with the death notifications
that may have triggered personal unresolved grief issues. Taking care of oneself is essential
when taking care of another person’s initial grief. The stages of grief and loss can certainly
take place among crisis counselors. When debriefing, it is important to take into consider-
ation that crisis counselors experience grief and should be encouraged to process that grief.
As a final activity, choose either Case A or Case B in Activity 5.6 and prepare a
death notification strategy as an individual, in small groups, or as a class. Substantial
modifications are often needed in schools and universities because the losses are more
likely to stem from suicide, accidental death, or death of a teacher or other staff. Also
read Voices from the Field 5.5.
Case A: Death Notification in a Community
Setting
You are part of a crisis team called to give a death
notification to the next of kin for a person killed during
a random shooting. You are not sure how the deceased
person was involved in or became a victim of the
shooting. Work with your team to plan what needs to
be addressed and then role-play a death notification to
the family.
Case B: Death Notification in a School Setting
The principal of your high school contacts you in the
morning and states that there was a phone message
left the night before that a ninth-grade student (John)
was killed in an automobile accident. As a counselor
and a member of the crisis response team for the
school, work with your team and decide what needs
to be addressed in this process and to whom
notifications will be made.

Chapter 5 • Loss, Grief, and Bereavement 	161
Maegan Vick
It was after school during a faculty meeting when the
police showed up. Most of the staff was already in the
meeting. I just happened to be in my office, about to
head that way, when I got called to the front office.
When I walked in, I noticed Eric, a first-grade student
who had suffered a traumatic brain injury (TBI) when
he was a toddler. Eric was still waiting with his teacher
for his father to pick him up in the front office. I said
hello to Eric and then noticed a police officer in the
corner speaking with the secretary. The police officer
introduced himself and walked with me to my office
without saying a word. I knew something was terribly
amiss. The police officer sat down with me and said
Eric’s father had been killed in a car accident on the
way to pick him up from school that day. This was espe-
cially tragic because Eric’s mother had been killed in a
car accident when Eric was a toddler. Eric was in the car
with his mother during that fatal accident and that was
how Eric got the TBI. Now, with both parents deceased,
Eric was all alone.
Eric’s teacher, Ms. Smith, waited anxiously in the
front office after she saw the police officer walk with me
to my office. As we returned, I watched her glistening
eyes search my face for any indication of what was
happening. When my eyes met hers, it was as if she
already knew her fear had come true. While Eric waited
in the office with the secretary, I pulled his teacher to
the hallway where we could be alone. “I’m sorry to
have to tell you this,” I said to her, “but Eric’s father
was killed in a car accident on the way to pick him up
today.” Ms. Smith sank to the floor with a muffled
scream, “No, no, no! It’s just not fair . . . his mother
was killed this way . . . I just . . . ” There were not
any words that could comfort her at that moment. All
I could do was tell her we would take care of Eric.
During the next couple of hours, we tried to contact
Eric’s closest relative, his uncle.
The following day Eric’s uncle, Matt, came to
meet with me. He was not sure how to tell Eric about
his father. It is hard enough for any first-grader to
understand that his mother and father are both dead,
much less a child with a TBI. I encouraged Eric’s uncle
to be as honest and as concrete as possible. He asked
me if we could tell Eric together, and we did.
“Daddy will not be coming home. He died. His
heart stopped beating. His eyes will not open. Daddy
loved you, but he will not be back.”
“Eric bad?” Eric asked. “Daddy mad?”
“No, you were not bad,” I said, “You are a good
boy. Daddy died. He cannot come back. He loved you
very much. You’ll go home with Uncle Matt, Jayne,
and Ally.”
Only the slightest sign of revelation appeared on
Eric’s face. “I go home with Jayne and Ally?”
We then read a short children’s story about
“Elmo remembering his daddy.” I gave this book to
Eric’s uncle so that Eric could read it at their house.
Matt called a few days later asking if Eric should attend
the funeral. I encouraged him to decide according to
which choice he and Eric felt most comfortable. Some
believe that allowing a young child to attend a funeral
can help provide closure. Matt took Eric to the funeral.
In the end, Eric adjusted well to his new home
and new school. His uncle grew more confident with
his ability to help Eric grieve and understand. Our
school learned to cope with a tragedy. I will always
remember Eric.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 5.5
A Death Notification in Elementary School
SUMMARY
This chapter reviewed several psychological
theories of grief, including Kübler-Ross’s
groundbreaking work on death and dying and the
task model put forth by Worden. The importance
of attachment across the lifespan was discussed
in relation to the work of Bowlby. More recently,
Walsh proposed a family systems perspective to
understand how a family member’s death affects
the entire family. Fowler’s faith development
theory and the FICA spiritual assessment tool
were introduced as means to explore the role of
spirituality, religion, and faith in the client’s life.
We are all born the same way, but each of
us dies differently. Death can come peacefully at
the end of a life well lived, or it can be untimely,
traumatic, or life-changing for the bereaved. As

-- 190 of 545 --

162 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
in the case of infertility, Alzheimer’s disease, or
missing persons, loss can also be ambiguous.
It is not possible in this space to create specific
interventions for every possible grief experience.
Rather, the goal is to help professionals learn
about grief and bereavement, be able to identify
the nuances differentiating normal from
complicated grief, and understand and address
any continuing issues of their own concerning
death and dying.
Type of relationship, attachment, cause of
death, and timing are unique to each situation
and require individually tailored counseling
responses. Professionals who work with crisis
intervention, and particularly death and dying,
should use basic communication skills to be
fully present and responsive to the needs
of the bereaved, to be open to familial and
cultural adaptations to loss, to encourage and
foster strength and resilience, and to consider
any spiritual or religious values that the client
has. The ultimate goal should be to provide
appropriate and timely interventions during
times of crisis so that people can experience and
process their grief in a healthy manner and not
become stuck in patterns that lead to depression,
complicated bereavement, or result in more
serious pathology. If successful, we will have
helped our clients forge a path through their
grief and once again find meaning in their lives.
Each person responds differently to a loss.
This chapter does not define major and minor
losses, as it is truly the perception of the loss for
the individual that will determine whether it
becomes a crisis. More importantly, counselors
should attempt to understand how a loss can
affect and cause other losses and how these
multiple losses are perceived when developing
a crisis plan or offering a referral. The DSM-5
task force cautions against attaching a premature
diagnosis to individuals who are experiencing
bereavement. The new diagnosis of other-
specified trauma- and stressor-related disorder
(specified by “persistent complex bereavement
disorder”) is discussed as an alternative for
complicated grief and bereavement and previous
diagnoses.
Death notifications are often viewed as
the least-favored duty for any professional.
Notifying someone of a loved one’s death can
be difficult, not only for the person receiving
the news but also for the person providing the
death notification. It is always important to be
sure that the person providing the notification
is not experiencing personal unresolved grief
issues and can provide a sense of calmness
and compassion. Death notifications can be
unpredictable, since no one knows how another
person may react in receiving such news.
Individuals may respond with a continuum
of 	responses—from 	numbness, 	excessive
sadness, and melancholy to anger. Unknown
circumstances may lead a person to project
their feelings on to the person providing the
notification. It is always important to understand
that reactions will differ and to keep your
personal safety in mind when providing a death
notification.
Although the likelihood of a counselor
being involved in providing a death notification
has increased over the years due to expanded
job responsibilities, research suggests that
counselors have little preparedness in this area.
Indeed, counselors have been involved in death
notification more often in their personal lives,
but this does not mean the process they used
was effective. Counseling programs offer little
to no formal training in this area. Knowing that
death is a natural part of the life process, it is an
appropriate part of clinical training to include
information on providing a death notification.
The level of preparedness of the professional
counselor in the notification process may affect
the reaction and the traumatic impact of the
delivery experienced by the survivor, reduce
professional burnout, affect the bereavement
process, and influence whether a person seeks
professional counseling (if needed) in the future.

-- 191 of 545 --

163
PREVIEW
Suicide and homicide are viewed as preventable global public health problems. Suicides
and homicides have a mental health impact on family members, friends, coworkers,
neighbors, and members of extended social networks, and even the professional
community struggles with the implications of these preventable deaths. Nonsuicidal
self-injury (NSSI) has always been a concern, but only recently has it received a similar
clinical focus and formal assessment to suicide and homicide. Even after inclusion in
the DSM-5-TR, assessment of NSSI occurs less frequently or is more targeted in certain
populations, like adolescents or persons diagnosed with borderline personality disorder.
However, an NSSI assessment should also be part of all suicide risk assessments and is
now required as a standard of care. Whether intervening before suicide, NSSI, or homicide
(prevention), while occurring (intervention), or after it has occurred (postvention),
professional counselors play a vital role in society’s response to helping those in need of
violence-free coping strategies, intervention based on sound practices, or, if necessary,
the best and most productive short-term and long-term response to survivors of suicide
or homicide. This chapter is explicitly for crisis counselors to learn how to be effective in
responding to members of their communities touched by suicide, NSSI, and homicide.
LEARNING OBJECTIVES
Upon completion of this chapter, readers will be able to:
6.1 Describe the prevalence and rates of suicide, homicide, and NSSI.
6.2 Describe risk factors, protective factors, and warning signs of suicide.
6.3 Distinguish between acute and chronic suicidal risk.
6.4 Describe how counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk as no, low, moderate, or high.
6.5 Describe effective questions and assessment strategies for suicide intervention.
6.6 Describe appropriate treatment planning and care management with a suicidal
client, including a safety plan, outpatient and inpatient treatment, and postvention.
6.7 Describe assessments and treatment for NSSI, including the use of a Q-tree.
6.8 Describe threat assessment and treatment options for intervention with people
who are homicidal or are threats to others.
6 Risk Assessment and Intervention:
Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury,
and Homicide
Judith Harrington, Lisa R. Jackson-Cherry, and Bradley T. Erford*
C H A P T E R
*We thank Charlotte Daughhetee for her contributions to previous editions of this chapter.

-- 192 of 545 --

164 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
PREVALENCE AND RATES OF SUICIDE, HOMICIDE, AND
NONSUICIDAL SELF-INJURY
Learning Objective 6.1 Describe the prevalence and rates of suicide, homicide,
and NSSI.
Clinicians and epidemiologists alike watch closely the rates and prevalence of suicide
and homicide to monitor the presence of conditions that may increase risk factors for
certain populations. (Nonsuicidal self-injury historically was not included in these
prevalence reports and more research needs to be conducted.) Counselors should know
what the terms prevalence and rate mean to interpret how members of certain at-risk
groups might require more attentive care:
• Prevalence is a nominal figure that states how many lives were lost to suicide (or
any other type of malady). For example, the prevalence of suicide in County RST
was 75 persons, contrasted with County XYZ, which lost 33 persons.
• Rate or crude rate is a value explaining data that has been interpreted by leveling
the playing field, so to speak; in other words, if all members of a population (or
an age group, gender, or ethnic group) were divided into groups of 100,000, the
rate would indicate how many people died per 100,000 people (sometimes called
per capita). For example, County RST, a very populated county, lost 75 persons to
suicide or homicide, but its rate was 10.9 per 100,000, or 10.9. County XYZ, a small,
sparsely populated county, lost 16 persons to suicide or homicide, but its rate was
19.7 per 100,000, or 19.7. Due to the common denominator of 100,000, the higher
rate for County XYZ is considered more alarming, even though the prevalence of
suicide was smaller in the rural county.
Epidemiologists also study the causes of death and the rankings of these causes, and
they track the rates of suicide and homicide, as rates provide a more uniform view for
analysis to which prevention efforts may be targeted. For example, the suicide rate for
Americans age 65 years and older is approximately 18 in 100,000, but the prevalence
is lower than in other groups because the population number (or size of this group)
of older persons is smaller. A rate of 18 in 100,000 is considered alarming because it is
higher than the national overall rate. For suicide, the national rate is 14.5 per 100,000.
Professionals want to learn why persons in each group are more prone to die by suicide
or homicide. Youth have a lower rate of death than older Americans; however, suicide is
ranked as the second leading cause of death ranking among youth, and homicide is the
sixth and third leading cause of death among 0 to 14-year-olds and 15 to 24-year-olds,
respectively (Drapeau & McIntosh, 2020; World Life Expectancy, 2020). Cause of death
ranking is often illustrated in chart form listing the 10 highest prevalence of deaths per
cause (e.g., heart disease, malignancies, respiratory disease, liver disease, unintentional
injury).
Drapeau and McIntosh (2020) reported an estimated 1,124,125 annual suicide
attempts in the United States or 25 suicide attempts for every suicide. Cerel et al. (2014)
estimated that for every suicide, 147 individuals (survivors) are connected to the person
who died by suicide (6.6 million annually). When examining the vast effects of those
affected by suicides, mental health professionals must be prepared to serve the high
numbers of survivors.

-- 193 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	165
Suicide is a public health problem that all crisis counselors, regardless of setting,
must learn to assess and treat because of its prevalence in society. In the United States
in 2020, suicide claimed 45,979 lives, with about one person dying from suicide every
11.1 minutes (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [CDC], 2022a). Consider these
data:
• In 2020 in the United States, 12.2 million adults experienced thoughts of suicide
(CDC, 2022a).
• In 2020 in the United States, 3.2 million people planned to attempt suicide (CDC,
2022a).
• In 2020 in the United States, 1.2 million persons attempted and survived a suicide
(CDC, 2022a).
• Though suicide happens in all age groups and is the 10th leading cause of death
across all age brackets, it is the ninth leading cause of death in the United States
among persons age 10–64, and the second leading cause of death for youth age
10–14 and adults age 25–34 (CDC, 2022a).
• Men die from suicide at a ratio of 3.6 for every 1 loss of life to suicide among women
(American Association of Suicidology [AAS], 2021).
• Women attempt suicide more often than men, at a ratio of 3:1; men die more often
than women, mainly because of more lethal means (AAS, 2021).
• The impact of suicide loss has staggering personal and economic impact on
individuals and communities. The CDC (2022a) estimated an annual cost of
$70 billion in the United States from lifetime medical and work-related expenses
due to suicide attempts and suicide deaths.
• AAS (2021) estimated that 135 individuals are exposed to or affected by each suicide
death, which is 6.9 million persons annually in the United States.
• The rates of suicide per 100,000 persons across the United States ranged from
6.2 per 100,000 (Washington, DC) to 29.4 per 100,000 (Wyoming) (Drapeau &
McIntosh, 2020).
• Suicide rates have been increasing. From 2014 to 2019, the rates per 100,000 persons
climbed respectively from 13.4, 13.8, 13.9, 14.5, to 14.8, with a slight decrease in
2019, to 14.5.
Furthermore, more than 1.2 million nonfatal suicide attempts occurred in the United
States in 2020, sometimes resulting in permanent injury or disabling conditions and
sometimes imperiling a concerned person who tried to prevent the suicide from hap-
pening (CDC, 2022a).
For a worldwide analysis of suicide deaths and homicide deaths, consult the World
Health Organization (WHO, 2021c) and the World Population Review (2022). The num-
ber of suicides worldwide in 2019 was 703,000 (WHO, 2021c) and the number of homi-
cides globally in 2017 was 464,000 (World Population Review, 2022).
Nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) behaviors are less often reported in the literature and
often not the focus of a risk assessment unless it is the presenting problem. This does
not mean the prevalence of NSSI is not occurring at alarming rates. NSSI is reported to
occur among 13–17% of American adolescents and young adults (Swannell et al., 2014).
Furthermore, NSSI relates to other mental health issues including depression, anxiety,
posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and suicidal risk. The recent inclusion of NSSI

-- 194 of 545 --

166 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
behaviors in the fifth edition, text revision of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of
Mental Disorders (DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2022) reveals the need
for a thorough risk assessment that includes NSSI behaviors.
Effective risk prevention requires input from multiple systems and increased
involvement from governments; the professions of mental health, public health, and
medical health; law enforcement; schools, universities, and other institutions of learning;
the media; and community leaders. Effective risk assessment also begins with the ability
of the crisis counselor to initiate and ask direct questions about risk assessment. If the
crisis worker does not bring up any of these issues or feels uncomfortable bringing these
issues up, the client may not feel comfortable bringing up risk issues. The first step to
providing a competent risk assessment is to be aware of comfortability in asking about
risk assessment. Activity 6.1 provides structured practice in asking clients about suicide.
You can also use the same activity to practice assessing NSSI and homicidal risk.
Form a group in which participants stand in two lines
facing each other. At the direction of a leader, the
participants in line 1 ask their partners in line 2, “Are
you having thoughts of suicide?” The members of line
1 should then move to the right (or circle to the other
end of the line if they are at the far-right end of the line)
and ask the next partner the same question. Continue
doing this until all line 1 members have asked everyone
in line 2 the same question. Then repeat the activity, but
this time, every member of line 2 now asks members
in line 1, “Are you having thoughts of suicide?” After
the exercise, discuss together as a group the following
questions:
• Which role did you prefer: being the person
asked or being the person doing the asking?
Explain.
• Why do you think it is important to practice
asking the question, “Are you having thoughts
of suicide?”
• How do you feel about using the word suicide
or about looking someone in the eye and asking
directly about suicide?
• What are some other equally direct and
nonjudgmental ways that you could ask about
someone’s intention to self-injure?
• What has been your experience with suicide, both
personally and professionally? Consider what you
do and do not know about working with a client
in a suicidal crisis or emergency. What more do
you need to know before you work with a client
who is suicidal? What personal attitudes, values,
opinions, or views do you have about suicide?
ACTIVITY 6.1
Suicide Terminology
The use of terminology is important when discussing suicide since the language chosen
could reflect personal attitudes and affect those impacted by suicide. For example, the
phrase “committed suicide,” although once common, is less acceptable as it implies a
negative perspective of the person. Compare the phrase to “committed adultery” or
“committed murder,” as they often carry a negative view of the person who participated
in or “committed” the act. If we define a person solely by the label of a mental illness,
we diminish the whole person.
In comparison to other types of medical deaths, we often refer to the cause of
death rather than placing responsibility on the person, such as “cancer-related death”
or “died of cancer.” Following these guidelines, language such as “died by suicide,”
“suicide death,” or “took their own life” are more acceptable phrases as they offer a
more humanistic approach to those grieving the death of a suicide and those who may

-- 195 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	167
have attempted or taken their own life. Terminology now considered unacceptable has
been used for decades by professionals, so be patient and use this as a time to provide
educational guidance to those in the field who may still use what now is viewed as
unacceptable terminology. Professional literature will still include what is now viewed
as unacceptable language. Try using the examples in Table 6.1 of “acceptable language”
in place of what is now viewed as “unacceptable language” around suicide.
Many individuals are impacted by the loss of a loved one from suicide, so it is
essential to understand correct terminology when referring to those impacted by suicide
and with other professionals. Primary victims are the individuals who took their own life
or died by suicide. Attempt survivors are those persons who have attempted to take their
own life but did not die. Survivors are loved ones affected by the loss of a person who
died by suicide.
RISK FACTORS, PROTECTIVE FACTORS, AND WARNING SIGNS
Learning Objective 6.2 Describe risk factors, protective factors, and warning signs
of suicide.
Most often, counselors attempting to provide a thorough risk assessment for suicide
will assess and report only on the risk factors of a client experiencing thoughts of
suicide or who has attempted suicide. Although this is an essential part of the suicide
assessment, protective factors are just as clinically essential to explore because they
offer insights into what is actually protecting or preventing the individual from taking
their life. So specifically ask, “What has kept you from following through on taking
your life?”
Counselors should discern these protective factors from the overall assessment and
build them into the care management aspect of treatment. If a counselor only focuses
on the risk factors and does not integrate the protective factors, the level of risk and
lethality may not be accurate. Risk and protective factors are individualized and depend
on unique client circumstances. Therefore, no single risk or protective factor can be used
across clients to indicate certain lethality and risk. Rather, the entire assessment profile
helps a counselor determine the most effective client care management action.
Risk Factors
Risk factors increase the likelihood of a person experiencing suicidal thoughts or
behaviors. Risk factors are often seen as the absence of something (e.g., supports,
medication compliance, medical wellness, loss of loved ones) but may include underlying
Acceptable Language 	Language to Avoid
Died by/of suicide 	Committed/Completed suicide
Suicide death 	Successful attempt
Suicide attempt (attempt suicide) 	Unsuccessful attempt
Person experiencing suicidal thoughts or behaviors 	Suicide attempter
TABLE 6.1 Acceptable Language to Describe Suicide and Language to Avoid

-- 196 of 545 --

168 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
meaning-related factors (e.g., feelings of guilt/shame, abandonment, being a burden)
that often increase risk lethality.
Many special groups have a heightened risk for suicide. These include Native
Americans; members of the military or veterans; first responders; older persons; persons
with life-threatening depression and/or bipolar disorder; victims of bullying; and youth
who are lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning, and other (LGBTQ+). For these
groups, suicide death rates are higher than average for the population. It is important to
note that a certain group does not have traits that make them more suicidal; rather, other
external determinants elevate the risk that members of some groups may become self-
violent. These factors may include marginalization, trauma events or complex trauma,
poverty, and exposure to self-violence or violence, among other factors.
Although agencies and clinicians may have different ways to inquire about risk
factors, for the following discussion, risk factors are placed into three main categories:
health factors, environmental factors, and historical factors. These categories may have
some factors in common.
HEALTH FACTORS 	Health factors include medical or mental health concerns. Often,
physical illnesses such as a terminal disease may also affect a person emotionally and
existentially. Thus, such illnesses may become complex risk factors. For example, an
individual with cancer may not be able to work, may become dependent on others, and
this may affect their family (e.g., financially or via role changes). In addition, the
individual may perceive they have become a burden to family members for their care,
which may be a significant change from their previous role of taking care of others.
Therefore, it is important that counselors understand not only the surface risk factor
(medical diagnosis) but also other factors that may complicate the risk (e.g., financial,
job status, perception of being a burden on others, independence).
Some mental health concerns may also impact the risk for an individual. Often
when the client is not able to think through rational situations or is impulsive, a higher
risk of acting on suicidal thoughts exists. Mood disorders, schizophrenia, anxiety
disorders, substance use, and certain personality disorders may also present a higher
risk for suicidal behaviors.
ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS 	Environmental factors can include cultural, behavioral,
and personality issues. Cultural issues include, but are not limited to, those relating to
race/ethnicity, gender identity, socioeconomic status, political affiliation, religious/
spiritual affiliation, sexual orientation/identity, age, and sex. Other environmental
factors may include, but are not limited to:
• Hopelessness
• Impulsive or aggressive tendencies
• Family history of suicide
• Recent job or financial loss
• Recent loss of relationship
• Perceived rejection associated with personal meaning
• Access to lethal means
• Lack of social support and sense of isolation
• Stigma associated with asking for help
• Lack of health care, especially mental health and substance use disorder treatment

-- 197 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	169
• Cultural and religious beliefs, such as the belief that suicide is a noble resolution
of a personal dilemma
• Exposure to others who have died by suicide (in real life or via the media)
• Ability to be a part of one’s personal rescue plan from past suicide thoughts/
behaviors
HISTORICAL FACTORS 	Historical factors are unique to the client’s life and include, but
are not limited to, history of trauma or abuse, abandonment of social supports, family-
of-origin risk factors, previous suicide attempts, family history of suicide, or humiliating
past events that are still causing pain for the client.
Protective Factors
Protective factors make a client less likely to engage in suicidal behavior. Protective
factors can also promote resilience and ensure connectedness with others during diffi-
cult times, thereby making suicidal behaviors less likely. Often, the factors listed as risk
factors for some clients may also be protective factors. For example, a client’s religious
beliefs may be the reason the client is not following through on the plan to die by suicide.
Consider this statement made by a client, “I have not followed through because I do
not believe I would be joined with my other family members in heaven.” A counselor
would not say, “Yes, according to your beliefs, you may not go to heaven.” Rather, a
counselor may respond, “It sounds like being with your family after death is important
to you. Tell me more about your religious/spiritual beliefs and the importance of being
reunited with loved ones.”
Another protective factor may be a single mother who says, “I have
not acted on my suicidal thoughts because I would hate for the father of our
children, who has been abusive to me, to be given custody and abuse the
kids. I cannot do that to my children.” Her desire to make sure her children
are cared for is a protective factor. A counselor would not reply with, “Yes,
that would be quite selfish to have your children experience the abuse you
did because you decided to kill yourself.” Rather, a response to support
the protective factor may be, “It sounds like you do not want your children
to experience the same abuse from their father that you did, and you are
concerned that if you took your own life, they would not be cared for the
way you want to take care of them. Tell me more about your children and
what you want for them.”
Other protective factors include, but are not limited to:
• Hopefulness
• Restricted access to highly lethal means of suicide
• Strong connections to family, friends, social, and community supports
• Skills in problem solving, conflict resolution, handling problems in a nonviolent
way, and lack of impulsivity in past situations
• Ability to see alternatives, limited cognitive distortions, and less impulsivity
• Cultural or religious beliefs that discourage suicide and support self-preservation
• Medication compliance
• History of ability to solve problems and create solutions
• History of demonstrating skills for handling emotional crises

170 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
• Expression of concrete and detailed future plans
• Family commitments, such as raising children or caring for siblings
• Willingness to sign a no-harm contract
• Being resilient in past situations
• Having a sense of purpose or meaning
Warning Signs
Warning signs may be present prior to death by suicide but not recalled until after the
fact. Survivors may experience guilt in not intervening in the suicide, which may cause
more complicated grief issues. Other clients may not present with warning signs at all.
Often, individuals who do not present with warning signs are those who truly want to
die and do not want any intervention. In addition, some warning signs may be normal
behavior for the person who died and therefore may not be seen as a warning sign by
family, friends, and counselors.
Some warning signs may fall into risk factors. Aside from assessment of risk,
counselors should be aware of various warning signs that a client may present
and provide psychoeducation and information to family members and friends (as
appropriate), such as including possible things to look for when they know their loved
one has indicated they are experiencing thoughts of suicide. The following are common
warning signs of suicide that may lead to acute suicidal thoughts or behaviors requiring
immediate intervention (Rudd et al., 2006).
• Talking about wanting to die
• Researching methods of dying, preparations, or rehearsal of taking one’s own life
• Talking about feeling hopeless or having no purpose; self-hate statements
• Talking about feeling trapped or being in unbearable pain
• Talking about being a burden to others; recent hospital discharge
• Increasing use of alcohol or drugs
• Acting anxious, agitated, impulsive, or reckless, especially if this is not the norm
• Sleeping too little or too much
• Withdrawing or feeling isolated
• Showing rage or talking about seeking revenge
• Displaying extreme mood swings
• Cognitive, behavioral, or mood changes
ACUTE VERSUS CHRONIC SUICIDE RISK
Learning Objective 6.3 Distinguish between acute and chronic suicidal risk.
Acute suicidal risk is defined as an individual who reports or presents with a desire
to die in a foreseeable time frame—usually 24 hours or less—with statements about a
specific plan, intent, means, and method (Kleespies, 2000). Some counselors may allow
for a longer time period, but there is still a specific time frame. For example, a client
who has been experiencing chronic and complex grief may state, “I just cannot get over
my mother’s death. On Mother’s Day this year, I plan to end it all so I can be with my
mother.” It would still be appropriate to consider this as an acute risk situation and to
take appropriate measures to start the preventative suicidal assessment and intervention.

-- 199 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	171
Acute crisis refers to situations that require immediate professional intervention.
For example, Jobes (2016) concluded there is a strong relationship between acute risk
and acute crisis events, such as personal failures that may impact core meaning or
situations that are perceived as humiliating to the client. Any recent events need to be
assessed to determine whether the client perceives the events as humiliating. These
acute crises alone may increase the suicidal risk and lethality to a high level, resulting
in immediate and imminent risk. Examples include demotions at work, a relationship
breakup, legal situations, discovering a betrayal, foreclosure or encountering adverse
financial situations, educational failure, and medical issues that may be perceived to
cause a financial burden on family members. Often, a humiliating event can cause an
individual to not think clearly and, combined with impulsive personalities, can trigger
suicidal behaviors.
Chronic suicidal risk is often connected with long-term patterns of relationship
difficulties, interpersonal conflicts, poor decision making in various aspects of life,
and repetitive destructive patterns causing life situation difficulties. These chronic
risk situations may be associated with chronic suicidal thoughts and possibly chronic
behaviors, but individuals may have less specific plans and less lethal intent or means.
Thoughts of suicide may last over long periods of time and may not appear as imminent
or immediate. Chronic risk must be assessed for the possible emergence of active or
acute risk.
Individuals with chronic risk can be described as having “markers” of a potential
suicide risk that is not currently imminent. In other words, individuals may present with
some identifiers of persons who could become acutely suicidal but are not currently in
imminent danger. Just as with other medical diagnoses, the client in your office with a
family history of cancer (a risk factor) may not ever be stricken with cancer. A person
who is chronically in suicide risk may thus have markers or risk factors for potential
suicide, but there is a chance the person will never attempt suicide. Chronic risk should
be observed closely for the possible emergence of active or acute risk. A few of these
chronic markers might include the following:
• Being from a high-risk suicidal group
• Diagnoses including but not limited to mood disorders (depression or bipolar
disorder), anxiety, eating disorder, thought disorder, substance use disorder or
some personality disorders
• History of trauma, abuse, violence, self-injury, previous hospitalizations, low self-
esteem, high self-hate, tolerance and acceptance toward suicide, oppression related
to sexual orientation, perfectionism, divorced parents, family history of suicide
attempts or fatalities, and other factors
In summary, chronic risk suggests the presence of longer-term potential of
suicidality, but with less specificity of intent or plans (e.g., means or methods) to carry out
an imminent attempt. The counselor has more time to help the client mitigate, manage,
or resolve psychological pain (and the need to end the pain), can be less directive,
and may not resort to immediate protection such as hospitalization as a treatment
strategy. The counselor will instead reassess suicidality regularly, manage safety, and
attempt to stabilize long-term risk with effective counseling. Table 6.2 provides further
differentiation between the acute and chronic risk categories.

-- 200 of 545 --

172 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
LEVEL OF SUICIDAL RISK
Learning Objective 6.4 Describe how counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk as no,
low, moderate, or high.
The gathering of information regarding risk factors, protective factors, warning signs,
and acute versus chronic risk will likely render a decision connected with lethality. Most
counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk on a continuum of low, moderate, or high:
• Low suicidal risk includes the presence of suicidal ideation/behaviors or infrequent
thoughts without a plan and means to carry out the plan.
• Moderate suicidal risk includes serious thoughts and desire to die with ideas of a
plan and means, often no access, some existence of social support, but also some
risk factors.
• High suicidal risk includes details of a plan and means to carry out the plan,
presence of risk factors, and absence of protective factors including support.
Although many scales indicate low, moderate, and high, there should also be a note if
there is no (or none) suicidal risk detected. Indicating “no or none” is a best practice
and allows you and other referring counselors to have a better comparison of suicidal
thoughts and behaviors over time.
Table 6.3 presents a sample rubric from an AAS-accredited crisis center utilized
for crisis calls and distance counseling to assess the level of suicidal risk of callers

experiencing suicidal thoughts. Law enforcement “hostage negotiators” also use
variations of this guide for crisis response teams.
In addition, the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (NSPL; 2007) recommends
following a rubric of assessing suicide risk based on the individual’s desire, capability
to attempt, and intent to die or attempt suicide.
Suicidal desire is indicated by:
• Desire to harm self or others
• Psychological pain such as hopelessness, helplessness, or feeling trapped
• Perceiving oneself to be a burden on others
Suicidal capability is evidenced by:
• History of suicide attempts
• Exposure to someone else’s death by suicide
• History of or even current violent behaviors toward oneself or others
• Having the means to hurt oneself or others
• Current intoxication or excessive substance use
• Recent dramatic mood changes or loss of orientation to reality
• Extreme agitation, rage, increased anxiety, decreased sleep, and recent acts or
threats of aggression
Suicidal intent is reflected by
• Attempt in progress
• Plan with a known method to hurt oneself or others
• Preparatory behaviors (e.g., giving away possessions, finalizing one’s business
affairs, saying goodbye [perhaps cryptically], counting pills, reading up on how to
use the method, loading and reloading the gun, rehearsing the method) and stated
intent to die

suicidal cases.

Kelson is a 22-year-old who has recently spent 27 months in a conflict zone. Kelson

The field has produced a variety of acronyms to assist counselors in recalling risk
and protective factors and warning signs during suicide assessments. These should not
serve to replace a formal model and Q-tree (which we discuss next) but may be helpful in
gathering information on risk and protective factors. The following is not an exhaustive
list but provides examples:
• IS PATH WARM (Juhnke et al., 2003): Ideation, Substances, Purpose, Anxiety,
Trapped, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Reckless, and Mood
• SIMPLE STEPS (McGlothlin, 2008): S—Are you Suicidal?, I—Ideation, M—
Means, P— Perturbation, L—Loss, E—Earlier attempts, S—Substance abuse,
T—Troubleshooting the client’s problem-solving ability, E—Emotion (hopeless,
hapless, worthless, loneliness, depression), P—Parents or family history/exposure,
S—Stress and life events
• MAN THIS ISN’T FAIR (Sankaranarayanan, 2007): M—Mental issues (depression,
anxiety, agitation, guilt, shame, delusions), A—Attempts at self-destruction, N—
No positive support from family/peers, T—Triggering stressors, H—Hopelessness,
I—Ideas and intent, S—Substance abuse, I—Illness/chronic pain, S—Suicide in
family history, N’T—(suicide) Note, F—Final arrangements/saying goodbye/
giving away possessions, A—Access to means, I— Isolation, R—Recent psychiatric
hospitalization
The above is only a guide to the factors that may affect risk and lethality.
There is no set number of indicators someone may have to determine lethality,
and a single indicator may not constitute higher risk. In other situations, an
individual may have many indicators but may not be high in lethality. In
still others, a few indicators together may be essential enough to qualify for
high risk. For example, if a client says they have a plan and means, have no
hope, and will follow through on their plan without seeking intervention,
then the individual may certainly have a high suicidal risk. There are many
assessments in the field that can be used to quantify the variables connected
with suicide.
EFFECTIVE QUESTIONS AND ASSESSMENT STRATEGIES
FOR SUICIDE INTERVENTION
Learning Objective 6.5 Describe effective questions and assessment strategies for
suicide intervention.
A suicide risk assessment should always begin at the first session in counseling, and it
should be the main assessment goal in any crisis. The counseling field has a plethora
of assessments and models for assessing suicide risk. A standard of care in our
profession is to not only provide a risk assessment of every person, regardless of the
client’s presenting problem, but also have a framework (model) upon which to base
the assessment. Although the profession does not dictate a specific suicide assessment
to use, it is a standard of care to use effective risk assessment to practice ethically and
reduce negligence.

Video Example 6.2
In this video, a counselor
works with a person who has
thoughts of wanting to take
his life. What are the risks and
warning signs and how would
you assess his level of lethality?
https://www.youtube.com/
watch?v=A-m_aIQfXZA
Watch from 00:00 to 12:05

-- 206 of 545 --

178 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
An effective suicide risk assessment relies heavily on clinical judgment and
interpretation of available facts, indicators, evidence of past behaviors, rating of current
mood and symptoms, and consideration of all these data against the backdrop of the
field’s knowledge of risk factors, protective factors, epidemiology, and causative factors
that are known to increase the risk of suicidal death. Regardless of the model used, there
are a set of questions that should be integrated to weigh the risk and protective factors
and make a sound clinical impression of lethality to provide a treatment plan.
Question-Tree Approach for Suicide Assessment: Essential Questions
Regardless of the assessment used, certain questions should be part of the suicide
assessment. Depending on the answers, follow-up questions should be asked for a
comprehensive assessment. If a counselor only stays with a surface question and does not
follow up with additional questions, the assessment is not complete and the treatment
plan may be ineffective or inaccurate, leading the counselor to an incorrect lethality
level. Using a question-tree (Q-tree) approach allows the counselor to weigh the risk
and protective factors and formulate the most appropriate plan for a client experiencing
suicidal thoughts or behaviors (see Figure 6.1).
Notice that the risk and protective factors are included in the Q-tree. In addition,
some indicators may be difficult to assess by a question. Desire to die may not, by itself,
indicate an individual’s risk level. Rather, the counselor must assess how capable the
client is to follow through on the desire. Capability is defined by a sense of fearlessness
and impulsivity to follow through on a specific plan and the means available to follow
through on the plan to die or attempt suicide. Neither desire nor capability
predict active behaviors toward seeking death as strongly as intent (Kalafat
et al., 2005). Strong intent to die may lead to reduced motivation or reasons
for living and can result in a more lethal method selection.
The recommendations issued by the NSPL (2007) include exploring
buffers, which are like protective factors to lessen suicidal risk. These may
include (1) immediate supports, (2) social supports, (3) hopefulness and
future plans, (4) engaging in the therapeutic process, (5) desire to live, (6)
core values and beliefs that conflict with suicide, and (7) a sense of meaning/
purpose.
TRIAGE APPROACH 	While using any suicide assessment or Q-tree,
counselors must also be observant and use the TRIAGE approach.
Counselors should observe a client’s affective factors (e.g., nonverbals such as
being withdrawn, uncooperative, distant, disinterested in taking part in a
plan), behavioral factors (e.g., how likely a client is to act on their thoughts or
follow through on a plan to stay alive), and cognitive factors (e.g., Does a
client seem rigid in their thought process or open to exploring the situation
and options for life? Are thoughts distorted?). In addition, developmental
factors (e.g., the perception of death as related to a client’s understanding of
death by age, cognitive level, or developmental abilities and limitations) and
environmental factors (e.g., various cultural values important to the individual)
should be gathered and documented. See Chapter 1 for more details.
