Suicide Lethality Assessments
Many assessments are available in the field and used as frameworks by crisis counselors
in lethality assessment. It is a standard of care to integrate at least one model into your
practice to provide documentation for each client. Regardless of the model selected, the
Q-tree discussed above should be integrated into any of these models. Some models will
indicate a section within the model for gathering risk and protective factors, although the
Q-tree or essential questions are not written into the model. The following assessments
provide a sampling from the field.
BECK SCALE FOR SUICIDE IDEATION 	The Beck Scale for Suicide Ideation (BSI) is a self-
report instrument requiring 10 minutes to administer that evaluates a client’s thoughts,
plans, and intentions to die by suicide. The 21 items are administered by the counselor
using a rating scale that includes items such as “I have made plans to die by suicide”
and “I have access to lethal means.” The instrument requires specific, detailed recall of
suicidal experiences, such as “I was XX% sure I would die by suicide” and “I have only
felt suicidal (number of) times.”
CHRONOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT OF SUICIDE EVENTS 	The Chronological Assessment of
Suicide Events (CASE) model was developed by a former emergency department (ED)
doctor who observed firsthand the high prevalence of suicide risk and suggested there
was a window of opportunity where professionals could intervene when a client was
discharged from psychiatric admission to the ED or psychiatric hospitalization. His
concern was with the high demands on ED medical professionals and being able to bet-
ter assess suicidal risk. The CASE method focused on the immediate factors of the cli-
ent’s suicidal risk—the conditions immediately prior to the psychiatric emergency (6–8
weeks buildup or a pileup of distress), the influences prior to 6–8 weeks of buildup
(e.g., long-ago exposure to suicide, family history of suicide, traumatic events)—and
concludes with questions about the “now” of suicidal feelings and pending days ahead.
The approach emphasizes five sets of question-asking:
1. Shame attenuation (i.e., normalizing feelings of psychological suffering and the
immediate issues the client has experienced)
2. Behavioral incident specificity (e.g., “Where is the weapon?”, “And then what did
you do?”, “And after that, what did you do?”, “Who knows about your plans?”)
3. Gentle assumption (e.g., “How have you thought of killing yourself?” rather than
“Have you ever thought about killing yourself?”)
4. Symptom amplification (e.g., “How much time do you spend thinking about
suicide in a 24-hour period?”)
5. Denial of the specific (e.g., asking clients if they have considered other methods—
this will not give clients the idea of having more options)
COLLABORATIVE ASSESSMENT FOR MANAGING SUICIDE 	Jobes (2016) developed an
assessment that combines the interview with the written instrument tool. Collaborative
Assessment for Managing Suicide (CAMS) is evidence and theory based and requires
close collaboration with the client to understand the details of the suicidal risk. CAMS
integrates safety and treatment planning and has been integrated into a 4-hour online
training. It can be found at http://www.empathosresources.com (Empathos, 2015).

-- 209 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	181
COLUMBIA-SUICIDE SEVERITY RATING SCALE (C-SSRS) 	The Columbia-Suicide Severity
Rating Scale (C-SSRS; Posner et al., 2011) includes detailed categories of suicide ideation,
intensity of ideation, and suicidal behavior to assess lethality of suicide. The scale has
been adapted and developed for various populations (e.g., general population, schools,
and first responders) and has suggested actions coded as red, yellow, and green based
on the responses of the individual.
MINI-MENTAL STATUS EXAMINATION (MMSE) 	The Mini-Mental Status Examination
(MMSE) is a brief (10- to 15-minute), quantitative measure to assess cognitive
impairment for those age 18–85 years, is available in over 70 languages, and is
recommended as a screening tool for depressed individuals (Folstein et al., 2016). The
MMSE demonstrated score validity and reliability in psychiatric, neurologic, geriatric,
and other medical populations. It is used to evaluate five areas of mental status:
orientation, registration, attention and calculation, recall, and language. The maximum
score is 30, and a score of 23 or lower demonstrates a level of cognitive impairment
indicating a referral for a more intensive cognitive evaluation. Orientation, place, and
recall are not impacted by level of education and are important to assess prior to
administering the examination. If only assessing for orientation, place, and recall, a
total maximum score is 17. The MMSE is attributed to the following descriptors that are
frequently referred to in many client charts:
• Oriented *3 (person, place, time)
• Oriented *4 (person, place, time, situation/event)
POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SUICIDE IDEATION INVENTORY 	Positive and Negative Suicide
Ideation Inventory (PANSI) is a 20-item self-report measure of positive and negative
thoughts related to suicide. It contains statements such as “I am in control of my life”
and “I think about killing myself,” to which clients respond on a 1- to 5-point Likert-
type response scale. This instrument is intended to be used as a screening tool for adults
and high school students.
SUICIDE ASSESSMENT FIVE-STEP EVALUATION AND TRIAGE 	The Suicide Assessment
Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (SAFE-T) can be used by mental health professionals
during the initial contact with a person experiencing thoughts of suicide. The five-
step assessment includes identification of risk and protective factors; conducting an
inquiry about suicidality (implementing a Q-tree); determining level of risk and
selecting an appropriate intervention; and documenting the process, including a
follow-up plan.
TASK MODEL OF CRISIS ASSESSMENT AND INTERVENTION 	The task model of crisis
assessment and intervention (see Table 6.4 and Chapter 1; see also Jackson-Cherry,
2018) can be integrated for a suicide assessment and focuses on four important areas of
crisis assessment leading to a more effective and appropriate four-task intervention plan
for individuals in virtually every type of crisis, regardless of setting. Assessment of
safety/stabilization; biopsychosocial/spiritual elements; clarification of the problem(s);
and coping skills, resources, and supports are essential first steps toward more accu-
rately defining the actual crisis situation.

-- 210 of 545 --

182 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
TREATMENT PLANNING AND CARE MANAGEMENT WITH A
SUICIDAL CLIENT
Learning Objective 6.6 Describe appropriate treatment planning and care
management with a suicidal client, including a safety plan, outpatient and inpatient
treatment, and postvention.
After assessing a client’s acute or chronic risk and determining the level of risk, many mental
health professionals might again ask, “Now what do I do?” Assessment is only part of the
intervention process. Rudd et al. (2004) and Jobes (2016), among others, provide qualified
step-by-step recommendations for appropriate treatment planning and responding. Just as
all treatment plans for clients seeking counseling for any condition are customized to meet
their needs, the same is true for clients at risk for suicide. The assessment process yields
valuable information to assist with the design of appropriate treatment interventions. This
section reviews several ideas for counselors to consider, beginning perhaps with the most
important intervention of all: the development of a customized safety plan.
Safety Plan
Developing a safety plan with the client is an industry standard and expectation, but
many mental health professionals were trained prior to the emergence of research
and data to write a “no-harm contract.” Based on research, best-practice thinking,
examination of the potential for false security regarding liability, and more evolved
legal, ethical, and clinical reasoning about how much a suicidal client might be able to
“agree” to such a “contract,” the no-harm contract has been replaced with professional
and empirical preference for the safety plan.
Even seasoned practitioners who are reluctant to do away with the no-harm
contract should be advised that not only is it ill-advised but it is also in contradiction
to many more modern insights about safety planning, and they could run the risk of
increasing liability if using no-harm contracts even in conjunction with safety plans.
Liability for knowing what to do in the case of a suicidal crisis is clearly the burden
of responsibility on the professional and not the client, who would be potentially
Level I: Four Essential Crisis Assessment Tasks
Assessment Task 1: Safety, Stabilization, and Risk
Assessment Task 2: A Holistic Biopsychosocial/Spiritual Assessment Approach (Integrate Q-Tree)
Assessment Task 3: Clarification of the Problem(s)
Assessment Task 4: Coping Skills, Resources, and Supports
Level II: Four Essential Crisis Intervention Tasks
Intervention Task 1: Normalize and Educate
Intervention Task 2: Exploration of Options
Intervention Task 3: Develop a Plan and Obtain a Commitment
Intervention Task 4: Documentation, Follow-up, and Referral
Source: Jackson-Cherry (2018)
TABLE 6.4 The Task Model of Crisis Assessment and Intervention

-- 211 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	183
considered not “competent” to direct treatment decisions if incapacitated with life-
threatening depression or multiple symptoms of any diagnosis. Many acutely at-risk
suicidal clients are not in a psychiatrically oriented or lucid condition to shoulder the
burden of knowing enough about suicide or safety planning. Therefore, the attempt of
the clinician to contract with the temporarily disabled client increases the counselor’s
liability through the use of a no-harm contract.
While some authors have referred to safety plans by varying nomenclature (such
as crisis response plan and self-care plan), the NSPL’s Standards, Training, and Practices
Committee recommends the term safety plan.
We would always use the term “safety plan” . . . other terms [crisis response
plan or self-care plan] are too vague and do not explicitly reference the fact
that keeping the individual safe is the primary goal in developing a plan—
vagueness is not good. In addition, we would never reference a “safety plan”
without explicitly stating the avoidance of anything that resembles a “no
harm contract”—so would attempt to eliminate any confusion at the outset.
(G. Murphy, personal communication, September 22, 2010)
Stanley and Brown (2009) have written extensively about the components of a
safety plan and recommend that clients ask the following questions:
1. When should I use the plan?
2. What can I do to calm/comfort myself if I am feeling suicidal?
3. What are my reasons for living?
4. Who can I talk to?
5. Who can I talk to if I need professional assistance?
6. How can I make my environment safe?
7. What should I do if I am still not feeling safe?
Stanley and Brown addressed not only what should be included in safety planning but
also how to talk through its use with clients. They are widely considered the field’s
experts on safety planning, and practitioners should explore their resources and seek
training in their Safety Planning Intervention.
A comprehensive safety plan addresses specific behavioral actions, determined
in concert with the client, and includes several key focal points including means
restriction, self-soothing options, self-care options, family involvement options, resource
development, and crisis response options (M. Gould & D. Jobes, personal communication,
November 10, 2010), in addition to the recommendations that Stanley and Brown (2009)
suggested.
One essential component of safety planning is addressing means restriction
(Coombs et al., 2010; Mann et al., 2005). Since firearms are the most frequently used
method with which to attempt suicide in the United States—and the most lethal with
the least amount of time for active rescue or intervention—counselors developing safety
plans should include a focus on means removal. It is best to engage a safety partner to
assist with means removal to guarantee the means restriction more fully, rather than
counting on the client to do so.
An example of a safety plan is presented in Table 6.5, written with samples of
specific behavioral actions.

184 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
A well-facilitated assessment will encourage a client to trust the counselor and
provide detailed information, which will allow the counselor to understand when,
how, and under what conditions the client becomes vulnerable to suicide. For example,
if a client reveals they want to end their life, particularly when experiencing conflict
with a partner, the treatment plan might recommend couples counseling and conflict-
resolution skills building. If an isolated 80-year-old widower reports being desperately
vulnerable between the hours of 5:00 and 8:00 p.m. each day when imagining all the
neighbors having dinner with their loved ones, a treatment plan should increase social
support during that period of vulnerability.
I will . . . (List specific behaviors such as the examples listed below). Customize the plan with
your client.
1. Remove gun and ammunition from house, car, truck, cabin, barn.
(Note to counselor: Means restriction)
2. Fill doctor’s prescriptions weekly (not a month at a time).
(Note to counselor: Means restriction)
3. Take my dog to play in the park.
(Note to counselor: Soothing feelings of distress)
4. Call a friend.
(Note to counselor: Soothing feelings of distress)
5. Eat three meals a day.
(Note to counselor: Self-care)
6. Drink nonalcoholic and noncaffeinated beverages.
(Note to counselor: Self-care)
7. Avoid lonely times by going to my sister’s.
(Note to counselor: Family support)
8. Spend the night at my cousin’s house.
(Note to counselor: Family support)
9. Go to a community support group for persons who have bipolar disorder.
(Note to counselor: Using resources)
10. Call my sponsor or go to a meeting.
(Note to counselor: Using resources)
11. Call 988 or contact https://988lifeline.org
(Note to counselor: Crisis or emergency support)
12. Go to my primary care physician, psychiatrist, or the ED.
(Note to counselor: Crisis or emergency support)
List the names and reliable phone numbers of several people that the counselor can call or who
can call the counselor in the event of an escalating situation:

“Don’t drink alcoholic or caffeinated beverages,” restate it in the affirmative “Drink nonalcoholic and
noncaffeinated beverages.”

Safety plans should always involve resources, actions, and coping
mechanisms that the client is able and willing to put into place, that are
specific to the situation, and that are written with clear behaviorally specific
verbiage. In the examples provided in Table 6.5, note that there are behavioral
recommendations for means restriction, self-soothing, self-care, family
support, use of community resources, and crisis response options.
Outpatient and Inpatient Treatment Decisions
and Management
Changes in the health care insurance industry and managed care have
severely reduced the amount of hospitalized care that at-risk persons may
obtain, even when suicidal risk remains high. However, when a client is in
immediate risk to self, the decision for hospitalization may be required to keep the per-
son safe. States and counties within states differ as to the process for involuntary and
voluntary hospitalizations, and it is essential that clinicians understand the laws and
policies in the state and county where their clients live. It is better to know what to do
prior to a crisis for both the clinician and the client.
Voluntary hospitalization is preferred over involuntary hospitalization as it allows
the client (and supports) to be part of the decision-making process. In many states, when
a person voluntarily seeks hospitalization, they may have more control in their discharge.
Although this may be preferred, voluntary hospitalization may leave the crisis worker
with an uneasiness of not knowing whether the client followed through with this plan.
When clients and families agree to voluntary hospitalization, the crisis counselor should
obtain a release of information and gain as much information as possible as to where
the client will be seeking admission so they can follow up. A well-being check can be
requested if there is any thought the client (or guardians) did not follow through. The well-
being check is a request of law enforcement to check on the “well-being” of the individual.
Since well-being checks do not fall in the priority of most law enforcement priority calls,
these checks may be at the discretion of the law enforcement officer. An involuntary
hospitalization may follow a voluntary decision that is not followed through by the client.
Involuntary hospitalization differs from state to state, whereas some states require
courts to mandate the commitment of an individual; others allow licensed professionals
to initiate an emergency petition for involuntary hospitalization; some states integrate
mobile crisis teams or crisis intervention teams, while others rely on a community service
board that would make the final assessment and determination based on the clinicians’
observations. The crisis counselors should also establish professional relationships with
community service officers in their jurisdiction and become familiar with protocols. In
these situations, once a client is involuntarily hospitalized, the client will likely not have
further contact with the crisis counselor, and clients will be discharged to outpatient
mental health counselors.
Partial hospitalizations are often referred to as a step down from a hospitalization
when a person may need additional or more intense support than outpatient mental
health. For some this may be residential support in the evening when risk is higher. For
others, like children, partial hospitalization may be scheduled during the day where they
are able to continue their schoolwork and access mental health services and be discharged
to parents in the evening. Medical stabilization and pharmacological intervention are

the goals of hospital mental health administrators, and discharge planning begins at
admission. No matter how difficult, uncomfortable, or disruptive a hospitalization is
for clients, it does save lives. Discharge planning and collaboration is essential between
hospital personnel and outpatient counselors to eradicate the high window of risk for
suicide after discharge.
CRISIS FOLLOW-UP CONTACT 	The follow-up with a client who is at risk for suicide is
critical practice (Murphy, 2010). Follow-up as a clinical standard is a form of active
postvention that is postventive, preventive, and interventive. Follow-ups may include
regularly scheduled phone calls, letters, or notes, and even texting or e-mailing, or they
may include memoranda of agreements with provider organizations (e.g., between
agencies or practices and hospital EDs).
Data about follow-up assessments with crisis center callers is very encouraging
in that callers who sought out suicide intervention assistance from centers are very
positive and motivated to reduce ambivalence about wanting to die. Clinicians may
direct family care team members to obtain helpful booklets from agencies such as the
Feeling Blue Suicide Prevention Council (https://www.givepulse.com/group/2786-Feeling-
Blue-Suicide-Prevention-Council?) to assist family members following a suicide attempt.
The Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration also has several
“after an attempt” resource booklets for medical providers, attempters, and family
members of attempters that can be obtained at https://store.samhsa.gov/product/A-Guide-
for-Taking-Care-of-Your-Family-Member-After-Treatment-in-the-Emergency-Department/
sma18-4357eng.
DOCUMENTATION OF LETHALITY 	It is important that the counselor notes what a client
reports and how they present. Not asking does not mean a client is not suicidal. It
means the standard of care was not met sufficiently. How a client presents is also
important documentation. Is the client agreeable to a plan? Are they more distant today
than other sessions? Do statements made by the client and how the client is behaving
conflict? Do they appear disoriented? Does the client have distorted thoughts?
A common risk statement should show that a verbal assessment was made as well as an
observation of nonverbal behaviors: “The client does not report or present with current
suicidal or homicidal thoughts and behaviors. The client denies a history of suicidal
behaviors or hospitalizations for psychiatric concerns.” This statement implies that the
counselor has asked the appropriate questions and the client has been evaluated based
on their behavioral presentation. This documentation should also be extended to NSSI
and homicide risk assessments. Now read Voices from the Field 6.1.
Amanda Evans
A common theme that I noticed when working at an

I can specifically remember several clients who were
misunderstood by the hospital staff due to their level of
cognitive functioning and were subsequently provided
more restrictive care. It is very important as a counselor
to be mindful that just because a client is an adult does
not mean that the client can manage the responsibilities
or cognitive complexity of adulthood. A patient who

Crisis Intervention with Clients Who Have Low Cognitive Functioning

Postvention
In recent years, postvention has grown to include the care practices that practitioners
provide to persons who have survived a suicide attempt. Postvention with attempt
survivors can include ongoing stabilization, long-term therapy, and improvement of
help-seeking skills and overall self-care, with a goal of long-term improvements in
quality of life and functioning.
Historically, postvention has been the type of care provided to survivors of suicide
loss. Survivors of suicide or homicide loss can develop protracted symptoms of grief,
depression, anxiety, PTSD, longer and more complicated grief and bereavement; more
demands on medical and mental health services; and greater potential for lost time
at work and at school. Survivors of suicide loss also have an elevated risk for suicide
attempts. The remainder of this section focuses on survivors of suicide loss.
BEREAVEMENT AND OTHER ISSUES 	Bereavement from both suicide and homicide can
remain complicated for many years. The DSM-5-TR (American Psychiatric Association,
2022) added a diagnosis of prolonged grief disorder; arguably, many survivors of suicide
or homicide loss might be at risk for this diagnosis due to the vexing circumstances
compared to other types of loss.
Given the prevailing prevalence of suicide in the United States, a conservative
estimate of persons who become newly bereaved due to the loss of a loved one by suicide
numbers over 275,000 per year (Suicide Awareness Voices of Education, 2022). The job of
bereavement counselors is an important one, due both to the large number of survivors
of suicide loss in the population and the misperceptions and myths about suicide both
before and after death. Survivors of suicide loss often report feeling misunderstood,
stigmatized, and marginalized. They are prone to bereavement and sorrow, complicated
grief, and posttraumatic symptoms (Harrington, 2011). Their bereavement may be
punctuated by the complexities of believing that their loved one “rationally chose”
death, as contrasted with viewing suicide as a multifactorial process of debilitation and
vulnerability (Survivors of Loved Ones’ Suicides, 2006). They may have lost a child, a
appears to be 50 years old may in fact function cognitively
as an adolescent or as a child. This can be challenging in
a hospital setting when clients in crisis require immediate,
effective, and appropriate medical attention.
Oftentimes, patients who were lower functioning
were expected to cope and manage in the same
way as their higher-functioning peers. This included
maintaining personal hygiene, medication compliance,
following the unit’s rules, and establishing appropriate
boundaries with their peers. In addition, I noted
counselors who provided treatment to these clients
struggled with gathering intake information and
establishing aftercare goals. This difficulty in serving
lower-functioning patients was most evident in the
staff’s attempt to deescalate crises. In my opinion,
clients who are lower functioning are commonly
misinterpreted by helping professionals, leading to
unnecessary rapid escalation in crisis situations, physical
restraints, and possibly medical restraints.
Based on my personal experiences, I want to
encourage counselors-in-training to consider alternative
methods to communicate with clients in crisis. When
patients who have low cognitive functioning are in
crisis, expecting mature, rational decision making may
be unrealistic; however, helping clients to express
themselves, calm down, or self-soothe is ideal. In
addition to talking through a crisis, working with
individuals using pictures, schedules, sign language,
silence, or techniques specific to the client’s needs
can be very helpful in deescalating a crisis. At times,
competent care requires stepping outside of the box
and using alternative interventions to best assist clients.

-- 216 of 545 --

188 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
sibling, or a partner within a cloud of unknown circumstances, such as losses or defeats
that resulted in acute suicide risk, such as a bad business deal, a negative performance
evaluation at work, or an act of infidelity that they could not work through, to name a few.
The circumstances surrounding the immediate scene of a suicide may include the
involvement of law enforcement, investigators treating the scene as a murder, potential
arrest warrants being issued, and limited or potentially gruff communication with
personnel such as coroners or first responders, possibly punctuated with yellow crime
scene tape. Of course, many survivors report exemplary conduct on the part of these
professionals, and so it is not a universal problem, but for some survivors of suicide loss,
the circumstances surrounding the immediate scene complicate the grieving process.
Adults and children alike are often witnesses to the actual suicide, they may
discover the body, or they might be involved in clean-up. Many family-related and
religious complications can ensue, with conflicts possible among stepfamily, family-of-
origin, or in-law family members who may disagree about stating the cause of death
openly to their communities. Sometimes the suicide may be like one domino in a row
of dominoes that continue to fall. For example, consider the scenario in which a middle-
aged person dies from suicide, leaving financial problems that result in foreclosure
or bankruptcy for the surviving family members, leaving college-age children unable
to continue their schooling, and a partner/parent who must reenter the workforce or
sell the house. The suicide can become one crisis of many in a sequence of events, not
unlike the ABC-X model of crises (Hill, 1949, 1958) or the Double ABC-X model of crises
(McCubbin & Patterson, 1982).
Counselors providing postvention grief services to survivors of loss should
become familiar with complicated bereavement and mourning, learn about counseling
techniques and countenance directly related to working with suicide loss, and especially
be aware of the feelings of helplessness, sense of responsibility, and internalized guilt
that the bereft feel after a suicide. Many family members have knowledge of their loved
one’s struggles and may have assisted in ways that in fact prolonged the life of their
loved one. Yet the traumatic circumstances, the stigma or belief that it was a rational
choice, or the complicated mourning leave survivors clinging to a sense of guilt-framed
attribution of responsibility.
FRAMEWORK TO INTERVENE WITH SURVIVORS 	Whether working with an individual or
with a group, there are common factors to take into consideration that may be unique to
those grieving the loss due to suicide: (1) prevention of further suicides, (2) reconstructing
the “why,” (3) commemorating the positive, (4) saying goodbye, and (5) an assessment
of guilt/shame and absolution of guilt. The main priority throughout the time with
survivors is to assess and prevent further additional suicidal behaviors. Often, suicide
survivors have no closure and may not know why their loved one decided to take their
own life. It is unlike the grief process during which a person is grieving the loss of a
loved one with a known cause (e.g., terminal illness, accident).
In suicide cases, survivors often are stuck in the “why.” Questions such as “Why
did he not come to me for help?” and “Why did she take her own life?” are things
that keep a survivor progressing through healthy grief work. Counselors may need
to normalize the repetition of these questions and reconstruct the why to understand
what the underlying meaning of these “why” questions is for the survivors. Meaning
may relate to the perception of being a “bad” parent, grieving the loss of a role of being

-- 217 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	189
a family member, or could uncover some hidden guilt in not being present when they
now believe they could have assisted in suicide prevention.
Connected with being stuck in the “why,” survivors are unable to have closure and
move on with their own lives through letting go or saying goodbye. Counselors, when
reconstructing the “why,” will often help clients work toward moving on in their own
life while still remembering the loved one lost. Similar to staying stuck in the cognitive
“why,” survivors may experience a range of emotions, including anger, sadness,
confusion, numbness, and guilt (to name a few) toward their loved one. Being stuck in
one emotion can also prevent survivors from moving on and healing in grief work and
may lead to negative feelings toward their loved one. Being able to commemorate a
positive trait or attribute can allow a survivor to start working through the loss.
Finally, in suicide cases, there may be a degree of guilt by the survivor, thinking
they could have done something to prevent the suicide. In many cases, survivors can
recall the warning signs of the loved one. However, these warning signs are often
only evident after the fact and could not have predicted a suicide. Therefore, it may be
important for survivors to know that whatever the situation or relationship leading up to
the suicide, it was ultimately the decision of the loved one to take their own life—offering
absolution of guilt. Having these aspects built into the grief process can help survivors
move into healthy grieving.
When working with family and friends, it is imperative to determine who may
have found the body. Often, a family member may not be forthcoming if they found the
body. The human senses (visual, hearing, touch, or smell) connected with the suicide
may trigger flashbacks for this individual; therefore, the individual who found the body
should be a priority in counseling.
Counselors should become familiar with how to find suicide bereavement
groups that exist in many localities around the country by visiting either the American
Foundation for Suicide Prevention or the American Association of Suicidology websites
for comprehensive listings. While survivors of suicide loss often report that they speak
the same language, a language that other bereft persons do not fully understand, not
all survivors will think that a group is the best option for them. Other suggestions
for therapeutic responses that counselors can recommend include bibliotherapy and
Internet resources, psychoeducation, family guidance, survivor outreach, cyber groups
or chatrooms, survivor conferences, and activism, with activism marking a full circle
from postvention to prevention.
Suicide bereavement counselors might provide psychoeducational insights to
survivors of suicide loss. For example, the counselor may provide insight that experts do
not believe that suicide is a “rational choice,” that intent looks very much like a “choice”
when clinically they are very different, that suicide was not done to punish the survivors
but rather to end the pain or to alleviate the burden that the decedent perceives they were
(often a mistaken perspective). Counselors should be prepared to support survivors of
loss when survivors encounter misinformation, myths, stigma, and judgments about the
type of death that occurred. Some people, even well-meaning friends, say hurtful and
untrue things, such as “I can’t believe he would do such a selfish and hostile thing to
you” or “How tragic that she committed this sin; she’ll surely go to hell.”
Finally, some counselors become crisis advisors to employers, agencies, schools,
faith-based groups, and communities. These professionals should be familiar with the
national resources available for responsible communication with the media following

-- 218 of 545 --

190 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
a suicidal death. All the prominent national suicidological organizations have such
resources available on their websites for distribution to decision makers, spokespersons,
and even media representatives to use the public news forum responsibly, prevent
suicide contagion, and avoid glorifying suicide or misrepresenting knowledge about it.
Now complete Activity 6.2.
ACTIVITY 6.2
Think about how society sometimes normalizes
language that is violent or self-harming. Have
you ever heard friends in casual conversation use
phrases to describe their exasperation like “That’s
enough to make me wanna kill myself” or “I’m just
going to shoot myself if he doesn’t quit teasing
me”? Perhaps you have seen seemingly humorous
references to suicide in unexpected places.
For example, a restaurant may have a dessert called
“Suicide by Chocolate” or a corporate trainer may
talk about “career suicide.”
Public messaging is a powerful tool in promoting
initiatives that are clinically responsible and in the
interests of public health. It is unacceptable to refer to
a fatal suicide attempt as “successful.” Only prevention
can be considered successful.
How we unwittingly toss around the words and
concepts of suicide says something to the public. We
may unconsciously promote stigma and even set up
barriers for the public to learn and to trust that help
is available. They will not have to worry about being
crazy, a quitter, a loser, or a sinner if they understand
that health care professionals know that self-directed
violence is the result of involuntary and debilitating
attempts to end psychological pain. To be more
empowered and enlightened is to use language that
is nonjudgmental and inviting to persons who may be
struggling to be help-seeking.
What will you do the next time you hear a
so-called humorous reference to suicide? How would
you entertain the idea that generally people do not often
joke about other life-threatening illnesses, such as cancer,
cardiac arrest, or diabetes? We do not see “Cancer by
Chocolate” on menus. People do not say “she cancered”
herself. What small individual act of suicide prevention
advocacy will you do when you have the opportunity?
NONSUICIDAL SELF-INJURY
Learning Objective 6.7 Describe assessments and treatment for NSSI, including the
use of a Q-tree.
The DSM-5-TR added suicidal behavior and nonsuicidal self-injury into the “other
conditions that may be a focus of clinical attention” section. The inclusion of NSSI
should improve documentation of these behaviors to determine risk factors for suicidal
ideation and actions and provide more effective treatment planning options. NSSI is
the intentional, self-inflicted, immediate destruction of body tissue, without suicidal
intent, that is not viewed as culturally acceptable behavior. Self-injurious behaviors can
be performed on any part of the body but often injuries are done to the hands, wrists,
stomach, and thighs. The severity of NSSI can range from superficial wounds to long-
term disfigurement. In one study, 33% of people who reported engaging in self-injurious
behaviors reported they should have sought medical attention; however, only 6.5% in the
same study reported receiving medical attention for their wounds. Although the most
common NSSI behavior continues to be reported as cutting, other common behaviors or
methods of NSSI include, but are not limited to:
• Burning
• Skin picking
• Hair pulling

-- 219 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	191
• Banging or hitting the head or other body parts
• Rubbing objects into the skin
• Skin erasing or carving
• Interfering with wound healing
• Bone breaking
Most of the literature suggests that NSSI increases substantially in early adolescence
and decreases during late adolescence and early adulthood (Plener et al., 2015) with
more incidents of NSSI behaviors by females (Fox, 2015). Considering the prevalence of
self-injury among adolescents, the likelihood that counselors and medical professionals
will encounter NSSI is quite high. Historically, NSSI has been viewed as not linked
with suicidal intent, but most clinicians and researchers suggest that NSSI is a strong
predictor of future suicidal behavior. Scott et al. (2015) estimated that suicide attempts
increased fourfold for those who previously engaged in NSSI and suggested that both
suicide ideation and NSSI are known risk factors for suicidal behavior with increased
predictive risk for suicide attempts.
Assessments and Q-Tree
The high correlation of NSSI and future suicide attempts prompted the DSM-5-TR
(American Psychiatric Association, 2022) to add suicidal behavior and NSSI into section
2, “other conditions that may be a focus of clinical attention.” Furthermore, it is a
standard of care to integrate an NSSI assessment as part of all suicide risk assessments.
It is essential to understand the intentions of those who participate in NSSI to properly
assess intent, implement the most effective treatment strategy, or appropriately refer to
additional care. Every intake should have an NSSI assessment in addition to the suicide
and homicide risk assessment, even if NSSI is not a presenting issue. It would be better
to include a standard NSSI question than not ask and live with the consequences after
the fact. This could entail simply including the following:
• “Have you ever engaged in any nonsuicidal self-injurious behaviors?”
✓ If no, proceed to a suicidal risk assessment
✓ If yes, complete the NSSI assessment
Kruzan and Whitlock (2019) adapted the transtheoretical model (TTM) of behavior
change proposed by Prochaska and DiClemente to assess motivation for change. TTM
integrates the following while assessing motivation of change: (1) precontemplation
(with no intent to stop NSSI); (2) contemplation (intent to stop NSSI within 6 months);
(3) preparation (intention of taking steps to stop NSSI within next month); (4) action
(currently taking steps to stop NSSI); (5) maintenance (steps have been taken for more
than 6 months); and (6) termination (cessation of NSSI for over 3 years).
We have integrated a Q-tree into the TTM as an example for crisis counselors to
assess and identify a timeline of NSSI, risk factors, and motivation for change (protective
factors) to provide referral for treatment.
Have you self-injured in the last 6 months?
If YES (to the 6-month question), is self-injury something you would like to stop?
• If yes, have you taken steps to stop NSSI?
✓ If yes, explore ACTION STAGE and ACTION PLANNING

-- 220 of 545 --

192 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
• If no, do you plan to take steps to stop NSSI?
✓ If no, client is in the PRECONTEMPLATION STAGE (consider psychoedu-
cation)
✓ If yes, client is in the PREPARATION STAGE (explore ideas and establish
plan)
If NO (to the 6-month question), have you self-injured in the past YEAR?
• If yes, is self-injury something you would like to stop?
• If no, have you self-injured in the past 3 YEARS?
✓ If yes, explore MAINTENANCE STAGE
✓ If no, no action is recommended
A more comprehensive assessment should follow this Q-tree screening approach.
The TTM approach assesses the timeline and motivations for change based on the
motivation for change theory. It is limited to these assessment categories unless you
implement additional elements needed for a more thorough NSSI assessment. We
suggest that more elements should be included for a comprehensive NSSI assessment
and have included the following adapted NSSI assessment, based on the research and
clinical work of Wester and Trepal (2017), who also found a high correlation between
NSSI and suicidal attempts. Thus, it is best practice to conduct independent suicidal risk
and NSSI assessments.
According to Wester and Trepal (2017), the NSSI assessment should gather:
(1) basic information, (2) contextual information, and (3) functional/reasoning
information. Table 6.6 can serve as a Q-tree for NSSI, and we have included some Q-tree
questions that connect to Wester and Trepal’s indicators. Upon gathering this information
and assessing NSSI risk, a crisis counselor can provide effective referral for a client.
Basic Information 	Contextual Information 	Functional/Reasoning
1. What was the age at onset?
2. How did the NSSI begin? (Was
there a triggering situation or
event?)
3. When was the last time the client
engaged in NSSI? (Start with most
recent, in past week, month, 3
months, year, over their lifetime)
4. What methods are used to
engage? Have methods changed?
5. Where on the body is NSSI
targeted?
6. Is NSSI known by others or is it
secretive?
7. Are there any wounds that have
needed medical attention?
8. Has client ever been able to
cease NSSI?
1. When engaging, are
substances (drugs,
prescription medications,
alcohol) used?
2. Has client ever found
a wound they had no
recollection of inflicting?
3. Is client able to delay
engagement?
4. If not, has there ever
been a desire to delay
engagement?
5. Is there a geographical
location or setting
where the NSSI occurs?
6. Does client have
thoughts or ideations of
suicide as well?
What reason is NSSI
used to:
1. regulate emotions?
2. feel better?
3. connect to
oneself (decrease
numbness)?
4. socially withdraw or
isolate?
5. engage or belong?
6. control or influence
others?
7. fill a craving or
addiction?
8. communicate?
TABLE 6.6 Q-Tree Questions for NSSI

Just as with suicidal risk assessment, the counselor should weigh the risk factors
and protective factors to gather all the information required for treatment planning and
referral. As a note, often NSSI behaviors can serve as protective factors so the counselor
is cautioned to not eliminate NSSI behaviors without evaluating the impact of stopping
the NSSI behavior, or exploring the elimination, as this could increase suicidal thoughts
or behaviors. This model is more comprehensive in gathering and reviewing several key
pieces of information that facilitate referral for continued care.
NSSI assessment, like suicide and homicide risk assessment, often requires long-
term care, which may exceed the time a true crisis counselor can offer, unless the NSSI
treatment takes place in long-term counseling. Often, crisis counselors will gather the
information and then refer the person to appropriate long-term counseling. Using
the template above allows a counselor to gather the basic, contextual, and functional
information needed to provide a solid referral for treatment.
Treatment Approaches
Understanding the most effective treatment approaches for NSSI is also essential. The most
common therapeutic approaches for NSSI include dialectical behavior therapy, cognitive-
behavioral therapy, problem-solving therapy (rational problem-solving therapy),
and treatment for self-injurious behaviors (T-SIB). T-SIB was developed as a brief
nine-session intervention to treat NSSI behaviors in young adults without borderline
personality disorder (see Table 6.7). T-SIB can be used as a standalone treatment or an
adjunctive intervention and includes the components in Table 6.7 with psychoeducation
Session and Overview 	Description of the Session Content
1. 	Psychoeducation
and Addressing
Motivation
Psychoeducation of NSSI, orientation to treatment, motivational
interviewing, and other strategies to decrease ambivalence may be
used by counselor
2. 	Functional
Assessment (FA)
Assess NSSI behaviors, provide alternative coping strategies for NSSI
if appropriate, and provide homework to address the functional
assessment of NSSI (is the person getting what they intend from
NSSI behaviors?)
3. 	FA 	Explore times when NSSI is not engaged
4. 	FA and Differential
Reinforcement of
Alternative Behaviors
Explore behaviors that may be adopted as alternatives to NSSI
and provide homework to only list alternative strategies with clear
instructions not to try them yet
5–6. Differential
Reinforcement of
Alternative Behaviors
Evaluate impact of each alternative listed, revise list, homework
assigned to implement alternative behaviors
7–8. Individual Modules 	Individual sessions for each client depending on their function
of events to NSSI. May integrate distress tolerance, interpersonal
communication, cognitive distortions, and restructuring
9. Termination 	Review progress and address obstacles moving forward

and therapeutic interventions. Counselors know how stressful this work can be and are
reminded to receive training, practice self-care, and seek consultation and supervision
as needed. Next, read Voices from the Field 6.2.
Amanda C. La Guardia
After completing my bachelor’s degree, I started
working in the mental health field as a case manager
assigned to a small number of children and adolescents
who had been identified as “at risk” for being removed
from their homes and placed into foster care or long-
term inpatient facilities. My job was to help ensure
these youngsters stayed with their families. I was with
each family for up to 12 hours per week, which was far
more than their licensed counselors were. I frequently
felt that I did not have the training necessary to help
in the way that was needed (which is why I decided to
pursue my master’s in counseling).
One of the biggest challenges I faced with
many of my clients was treating non-suicidal self-
injurious behavior. I worked with several adolescent
females who used self-injury as a way of coping with
either the psychological impact of the trauma they
had experienced or their emotional turmoil. I also
encountered elementary school–age boys who engaged
in self-injurious behaviors for similar reasons. Rather than
perceiving these behaviors as a coping strategy, many of
the counselors I worked with judged these behaviors to
be the result of mental illness. For example, borderline
behavior was a consistent assessment for the females
and impulsivity because of hyperactivity was a common
assessment for the boys. Thus, I noticed clinicians often
did not focus on nonsuicidal self-injury as an important
treatment concern. I knew better, but I needed training.
In many cases, the clients who engaged in the
most frequent self-injurious behaviors were also con-
templating suicide. They would injure themselves to
deal with their overwhelming emotions (or lack of
emotional connection) and to prevent acting out on
their suicidal thoughts. This combination was tricky.
I knew they needed a better way of coping, but I did
not want to take away the method that they felt kept
them from walking down a more dangerous path.
Formal and informal ways of assessing their suicidal
ideations as well as their need for self-injury became
equally necessary. Self-injury and suicidal ideation are
separate issues, but they are often interconnected.
Together with my clients, we discussed what
situations (external and internal) tended to lead to
self-injurious behaviors and worked on developing
new ways of coping. I did not ask them to stop, but
I did ask them to take care of themselves and
encouraged them to find new ways of dealing with
their pain because they deserved happiness, however
they defined it; they were worth it. I let them know
I would be with them as they struggled, and then I
made sure that I was.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 6.2
Treating Nonsuicidal Self-Injury
HOMICIDE
Learning Objective 6.8 Describe threat assessment and treatment options for
intervention with people who are homicidal or are threats to others.
Most counselors are aware that they are likely to encounter suicidal clients in their coun-
seling practice (Laux, 2002) and be exposed to persons experiencing thoughts of suicide.
Still, many counselors underestimate the need for expertise in assessment and inter-
vention skills with homicidal clients. There is a general assumption that clients with
homicidal ideation are found primarily within the domain of forensic mental health and
therefore are not a concern for most counselors in a variety of settings. Obviously, clients
with a history of certain mental disorders and violent offenses do present an elevated
risk to the public, and mental health professionals working with violent offenders should
strive to balance public safety and client rights (Carroll et al., 2004; Large et al., 2011).

-- 223 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	195
Ongoing reports from the media reveal that the potential for violence and
homicide exists in families, schools, colleges, the workplace, and across all communities.
Accordingly, it is essential that counselors in all practice settings be equipped to assess
the lethality of clients with homicidal ideation and be capable of making decisions
regarding the referral and treatment of dangerous clients. Van Brunt (2015) stated
that while mental illness is often a contributing element, “the core of assessment must
be based on threat assessment principles, not clinical pathology” (p. viii). Moreover,
counselors in all settings may be called on to provide services to survivors of loss due to
homicide. A homicidal crisis is intense and has long-lasting repercussions for individuals
and communities who are left struggling to make sense of their traumatic loss and
complicated grief (Currier et al., 2006). In this section, we explore the issue of homicidal
ideation and systems of threat level assessment as well as treatment options.
Homicide can be defined as the willful killing of one person by another person.
According to the CDC (2022b), in 2018 there were 24,576 homicides in the United States
(7.5 per 100,000). The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI; 2019) reported 16,425 homi-
cides for 2019, which reflected a rate of 5.0 per 100,000 in that year. Preliminary analy-
sis forecasts that the downward trend will continue, though the trends will be studied
closely given any pandemic-related influences on the increase or decrease in violence.
It is also important to note that while overall homicide rates have been decreasing, the
rate of mass shootings has been on the rise, with 40 mass shootings in 2020, up from 30
in 2019 (FBI, 2021).
Sixty-eight percent of homicides are committed with guns (Doucette et al., 2019).
Mental health professionals as prevention advocates are faced with myriad complex
variables related to public opinions, political and values-informed discourse, the
availability of guns through legal and illegal means, conflicting social values between
personal freedoms and personal safety, and other factors. At the very least, within the
clinical relationship, clinicians can and should strive to prevent homicide by attending to
clients’ safety with means-removal, means-restriction, and safety planning. They should
work with highly attuned standards for duty to warn and duty to protect.
“I could just kill him!” That is not an uncommon declaration from a client venting
anger at a difficult partner, peer, or coworker, and counselors must be able to ascertain
frustration from true risk of violence (Welfel, 2016). Such threats of harm toward another
usually transpire when a client is experiencing extreme psychological anguish and is
attempting to resolve feelings of distress; they are expressions of emotion without any
real intent to carry out a threat (Warren et al., 2011). The counselor is then tasked with
determining whether such pronouncements are simply common idiomatic expressions
of anger or are legitimate lethal threats. Fortunately, in most instances, clients are merely
indulging in hyperbole to give voice to intense anger or frustration; however, counselors
must be prepared to recognize and assess potential lethality in cases where resentment
and infuriation have escalated into homicidal ideation and possible violent actions.
Legal Issues
Working with potentially violent and homicidal clients presents a challenge to crisis
counselors as they attempt to assess possible homicidal behavior, provide treatment, and
protect potential intended victims and themselves (Meloy et al., 2011). When counseling
potentially homicidal clients, the protection of the public (or individual) is the priority.

-- 224 of 545 --

196 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
The noteworthy 1976 case of Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California (refer to
Chapter 3) clarified that the duty under California law to warn individuals who are in
peril is of greater importance than the preservation of client confidentiality (Rothstein,
2014; Welfel, 2016). The Tarasoff case established the legal construct of duty to warn
through case law that inspired a higher ethical standard, even though in many states it
was not codified by law and still may not be used as a precedent in some states.
It is a good idea for crisis counselors to become familiar with the regulations of
their own state laws regarding the duty to warn, as these issues are constantly being
addressed by legislators at the state level. Welfel (2016) noted that while codes of ethics
allow breach of confidentiality regarding dangerous clients and the burden of responsi-
bility to warn targeted victims, there are state laws that require client protection to take
place through hospitalization of the threatening individual (e.g., in Ohio) or contacting
police rather than directly contacting the intended victim (i.e., in Texas) and breaching
confidentiality.
Counselors must be aware of the terminology in their licensing states in relation to
homicidal behaviors. For example, Welfel reports that some states clarify that the harm
must be imminent before confidentiality can be breached and may require consultation
with other mental health professionals. Legal consultation is strongly urged before
confidentiality is breached.
Assessment of Homicide: Risk Factors
Certain basic indicators and risk factors tend to be associated with homicidal behavior
and violence. However, a word of caution about risk factors: While they may assist
crisis counselors in assessment, it is important to remember that the existence of risk
factors does not mean the client will become violent, and, conversely, the absence of
risk factors does not indicate a lack of homicidal intention. Remember, each case must be
evaluated on its own terms. With or without the presence of certain risk factors, someone
can become homicidal at any time for any reason. Just as the existence of a risk factor
cannot predict suicidality, risk factors for homicide in clients cannot predict whether the
client will act in a violent manner (Van Brunt, 2015).
The FBI Unified Crime Report (2019) reports the following:
• 78.3% of the 13,927 murder victims were male.
• When race was provided for the 13,927 murder victims, 54.7% were Black, 42.3% were
White, and 3.1% were of other races. Data on race was not included for 234 victims.
• More than 73% of the homicides involved the use of firearms.
• 28.3% of homicide victims were killed by someone they knew other than a family
member (acquaintance, neighbor, friend, boyfriend, etc.), 13.0% died at the hands
of a family member and 9.9% were murdered by a stranger.
Berghuis and Klott (2015) noted that, among other behaviors, males who become
homicidal tend to exhibit impulsivity and have prevalence of mental illness, family
violence, job instability, and overall insecurity in their social contexts. They usually
express a need to control intimate relationships and may exhibit possessiveness and
rage. The need to control others is most clearly demonstrated in domestic violence cases
where batterers control their partners through threats, physical violence, isolation from
friends and family, and limiting access to money. Emotional reactivity and acting out

-- 225 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	197
of jealousy and revenge were found to be common themes among domestic violence
perpetrators (Juodis et al., 2014a).
Research provides information about the characteristics and risk factors associated
with youth who commit acts of violence and homicide. According to the CDC (2023a),
homicide is the third leading cause of death for youth between the ages of 10 and 24 and
the leading cause of death for non-Hispanic Black youth. Sexual minority teens are more
likely to experience multiple forms of violence compared to their heterosexual peers.
Furthermore, the report outlines various risk and protective factors, although it cautions
that risk factors are not direct causes of youth violence. A combination of individual,
relationship, community, and societal factors contribute to the risk and prevention of
youth violence.
• Individual risk factors: History of violence victimization; attention deficit or
learning disorders; history of early aggressive behavior; use of drugs, alcohol, or
tobacco; low IQ; poor behavior control; deficits in social cognitive or information-
processing abilities; intense emotional distress; history of treatment for emotional
problems; antisocial traits and beliefs; exposure to violence
• Family risk factors: Authoritarian parents; inconsistent disciplinary practices;
low parental involvement; low emotional attachment to parents or caregivers; low
education and income of parents; substance use or criminal incidents by parents;
poor family functioning; lack of supervision of children
• Peer and social risk factors: Delinquent peers; involvement in gangs; social rejection
by peers; lack of involvement in acceptable activities; poor academic performance;
low commitment to school and school failure
• Community risk factors: Diminished economic opportunities; high level of tran-
siency; high level of family disruption; low levels of community participation;
socially disorganized neighborhoods
Protective factors would be the opposite of the risk factors listed above.
School Homicide and Violence
The Association for Supervision and Curriculum Development (ASCD; 2019) reminds
readers and members of K–12 school personnel that there is not a simple or obvious
profile of a student who might enact a school shooting. Though school shootings result
in extensive media coverage and collective trauma within communities and beyond,
the prevalence of gun violence off the school campus is still substantially higher
than on campuses. ASCD reported that the average number of deaths from gunshot
wounds between 2002 and 2014 was 1,300 per year, compared with 117 total fatalities
in school locations between 2000 and 2014. Despite the tragic and vexing aftermath
of school shootings, educational settings may be considered safer than other settings
that children frequent. Everyone who cares about safer communities and schools wants
to know who that child may be that could become a school shooter, yet the ability to
predict or “spot the profile” of such a youth is elusive. ASCD reported that, while most
school shooters are White male teens, their academic and behavioral histories were
unremarkable. They experienced some successes in school, had friends or a social group,
and were relatively typical, leading the FBI and the U.S. Secret Service to advise against
using profile analysis as a prevention approach.

-- 226 of 545 --

198 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
That said, in recent years, it has become tragically evident that K–12 schools and
higher-education settings are vulnerable to heinous acts of violence. The FBI (2019)
tracked active shooter events from 2000 to 2013 and found a steady increase over that
time span. Of those incidents, 24.4% occurred in educational settings. Moreover, the
incidents that occurred on campuses accounted for some of the highest casualty rates.
As with violent incidents in other settings, school shootings are precipitated
by a complex interplay of personal issues and environmental circumstances. Again,
it is important to note that the presence of certain risk factors cannot predict actual
violent acts, but since most violent students communicate their intentions, ongoing
threat assessment can help school officials prevent violence and provide intervention
to troubled students. By using systematic threat assessment and being attuned to
warning signs, educational settings can more clearly identify individuals with violent
intent. According to Meloy et al. (2011), “warning behaviors are acts which constitute
evidence of increasing or accelerating risk . . . acute, dynamic, and particularly
toxic changes in patterns of behavior which may aid in structuring a professional’s
judgment that an individual of concern now poses a threat” (p. 256). School personnel
and crisis staff are certainly encouraged to maximize any efforts within the social
and emotional development initiatives schoolwide to equip students with increasing
emotional self-awareness, strong coping skills, help-seeking skills, conflict resolution
skills, and more.
Meloy et al. (2011) developed a typology of eight warning behaviors that can indi-
cate amplified risk of targeted violence and should be utilized when assessing a threat.
They state that “threat assessment is concerned almost wholly with the risk of targeted
violence by a subject of concern and has a behavioral and observational policing focus”
(p. 257). The eight warning behaviors are as follows:
1. Pathway warning behavior—the individual plans an attack
2. Fixation warning behavior—the individual becomes obsessed with a person or cause
3. Identification warning behavior—the individual identifies with attackers or warriors
4. Novel aggression warning behavior—the individual tries out violent behavior as a test
5. Energy burst behavior—the individual increases activities related to the intended
victim(s)
6. Leakage warning behavior—the individual tells another person about the plans
7. Directly communicated threat warning behavior—the individual makes an explicit
threat to the target or police
8. Last resort warning behavior—the individual displays an increase in distress and
comes to believe that there is no other way forward but violence toward the target
(Meloy & O’Toole, 2011)
It is hoped that this typology of warning behaviors can make threat assessment more
systematized and usable.
According to Meloy and O’Toole (2011), individuals leak their plans for a variety of
reasons, such as excitement, attention, anxiety, or fear. Leakage may be unintentional or
intentional. If leakage occurs during counseling, the counselor should discern the need
to follow duty to warn responsibilities. O’Toole (2014) noted that individuals who are
withdrawn, detached from others, and think they have been attacked by another person
(whether the attack is real or imagined) may begin to plot a revenge attack. Erford et al.
(2019) also state that revenge for bullying or social rejection is a specific motivation for

-- 227 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	199
many school shootings. Bullying prevention must be considered an important facet of
any school violence prevention program (Duplechain & Morris, 2014). The U.S. Secret
Service (2010) offered this insight into the prevalence of leakage:
School-based attacks are rarely impulsive acts. Rather, they are typically
thought out and planned in advance. Almost every attacker had engaged in
behavior before the shooting that seriously concerned at least one adult—
and for many had concerned three or more adults. In addition, prior to most
of the incidents, other students knew the attack was to occur but did not
alert an adult.
This information points to the need to educate students, school staff and faculty, and the
public about leakage. Violence in school is usually planned, and school staff and faculty
need to be particularly attentive to signs and symptoms of violence as well as to school
environmental factors that give rise to bullying, rejection, and the promotion of violence
(Miller et al., 2000).
Kanan (2010) highlighted the following 10 findings on violent incidents in schools:
1. Incidents of violence are rarely impulsive acts.
2. Prior to most incidents, other people knew about the attacker’s ideas or plans.
3. Most attackers did not threaten their targets directly prior to advancing the attack.
4. There is no active or useful profile of students who engage in targeted school
violence.
5. Prior to the attack, most attackers engaged in some behavior that caused concern
or indicated a need for help.
6. Most attackers were known to have difficulty coping with significant losses or
personal failures.
7. Many attackers felt bullied, persecuted, or injured by others prior to the attack.
8. Most attackers had access to and had used weapons prior to the attack.
9. In many cases, other students were involved in some capacity.
10. Despite prompt law enforcement responses, most shooting incidents were stopped
by means other than law enforcement interventions. (p. 32)
A key point to consider in school shootings is the fact that attackers usually indicate
their intentions to someone. Most of the time, other students had some hints that a fellow
student was ready to lash out. Part of the difficulty that schools face is the fact that
students are reluctant to tell adults their suspicions. Payne and Delbert (2011) noted that
the use of an anonymous hotline for students to report threats, mistreatment, or bullying
can be an effective deterrent to incidents of school violence. Prevention messaging, such
as “if you see something, say something,” or training youth to get help from an adult
are important school context norms to advance.
In general, college campuses are safe environments, and there is a lower rate
of crime on college campuses compared to off-campus settings (Carr & Wood, 2006).
Horrific shootings on college campuses are more reflective of problems with mental
health access and compliance than of problems with campus safety. Colleges and
universities can combat violent incidents through training for faculty, staff, and even
students. The implementation of threat assessment and prevention programs on
campus and campus-wide warning systems could provide the means to thwart future
acts of violence and protect student lives. Campus leadership can continue to improve

-- 228 of 545 --

200 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
access to mental health care, foster social and emotional support in residential facilities,
and improve morale across groups (Van Brunt, 2015). Case Study 6.2 addresses the
importance of risk assessment in schools.
CASE STUDY 6.2
Risk Assessment in Schools
Peyton is a grade 10 student who is very withdrawn and has few friends. Dalton, the
school counselor, is concerned about Peyton. Last year there was a case where some
students were bullying Peyton, who then shared thoughts of suicidal ideation with
Dalton. Peyton has been seeing a counselor for the past year and the bullies were
punished; however, Dalton has a gut feeling that the bullying continues “under the
radar” of school officials. Peyton’s English teacher recently stopped by to show Dalton
one of Peyton’s tests. Peyton did not answer a single question, but instead drew pictures
of guns all over the test.
Discussion Questions
1. What should Dalton do?
2. What questions should Dalton ask?
3. What process should Dalton follow?
4. Are there any special considerations since they are in a school setting?
5. Are there any gender considerations?
Workplace Violence and Homicide
In 2017, there were 458 workplace homicides (Doucette et al., 2019) and, according
to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (2021), 20,870 workers in the private industry expe-
rienced trauma from nonfatal workplace violence in 2019. Of those victims who
experienced trauma from workplace violence:
• 68% were female
• 65% were age 25–54 years
• 70% worked in the health care and social assistance industry
• 21% required 31 or more days away from work to recover, and 20% needed
3–5 days away from work
However, the number of workplace homicides has decreased overall since 1993, a year
in which 1,068 workplace homicides occurred. Even with this decrease over the years,
homicide in the workplace is the fourth leading cause of fatality at work and remains a
disturbing reality. Most workplace homicides involve robberies, and individuals who
work in retail and hospitality establishments are at greater risk, as are customers and
bystanders in these settings. Approximately 80% of workplace fatalities result from
shootings.
Just as a school can be held liable for not addressing bullying and threats, the
workplace can also be liable for failing to address danger and threats (Lies & Simonsen,
2015). Lies and Simonsen recommend that workplaces have clearly stated policies

-- 229 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	201
regarding violence and threats, a way for employees to communicate concerns, a system
to promptly investigate concerns, a discipline policy, training for all employees on the
signs and symptoms of violent intent, and an Employee Assistance Program that can
counsel employees in distress to help prevent acts of violence.
According to the CDC (2021a), the most dangerous workplace risk factors involve
the following:
• Contact with the public
• Exchange of money
• Delivery of passengers, goods, or services
• Having a mobile workplace such as a taxicab or a police cruiser
• Working with unstable or volatile persons in health care, social service, or criminal
justice settings
• Working alone or in small numbers
• Working late at night or during early-morning hours
• Working in high-crime areas
• Guarding valuable property or possessions
• Working in community-based settings
The risk of workplace violence and homicide increases during times of extreme job
stress. Understaffed job sites, overworked employees, and times of downsizing or labor
disputes will create a tense and potentially dangerous context for the development of
violent behavior. Additionally, poor management, a high number of grievances, and the
lack of employee access to counseling can contribute to violence and homicidal threat
in the workplace (O’Toole, 2000). It is essential that workplace employers, particularly
in high-pressure workplaces or those undergoing turmoil, be mindful of the need for
ongoing threat assessment and be proactive by providing support to employees.
Threat Level Assessment
Various models of threat assessment have emerged over the past years, and it is beyond
the scope of this chapter to outline all of them. Counselors are encouraged to familiarize
themselves with a variety of models of threat assessment.
When a client has overtly indicated intent to harm others or has indirectly exhibited
predictors or behaviors of violence and harm to others, this constitutes a direct threat, and
the counselor must assess the level of threat (Warren et al., 2011). Threat level assessment
is critically important to ensure the safety of others and to appropriately guide treatment
decisions. While most people who make threats are unlikely to carry out a violent action,
all threats must be taken seriously and evaluated (O’Toole, 2000). Threat assessment is
the evaluation of the lethality of a threat through an examination of motive, risk factors,
intent, and the means and ability to enact the threat.
O’Toole (2000) explained that threat assessment should involve exploration of the
following questions: What are the details of the threat? Are the victims identified? Are
the details logical, plausible, and spelled out in a specific manner (time of day, method)?
Does the client have the means to carry out the threat? What is the emotional state of the
client? While emotionality does not specifically indicate lethality, knowing the affective
condition of the client provides important assessment and diagnostic information related
to lability of mood, dysregulation of feelings, ability to think clearly or not, rational

-- 230 of 545 --

202 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
functioning or not. Are there stressors or triggers that might predispose a client to
violence? Is the level of threat low, medium, or high? O’Toole, working with the FBI
National Center for the Analysis of Violent Crime, delineated threat levels as follows:
Low Level of Threat: A threat that poses a minimal risk to victim and public safety.
The threat is vague and indirect.
Information contained within the threat is inconsistent.
The threat lacks realism.
The content of the threat suggests the person is unlikely to carry it out.
Medium Level of Threat: A threat that could be carried out, although it may not
appear to be entirely realistic.
The threat is more direct and more concrete than a low-level threat.
The wording in the threat suggests that the person issuing the threat has given
some thought to how the act will be carried out.
There may be a general indication of a possible place and time (though these
signs still fall well short of a detailed plan).
There is no strong indication that the person who issued the threat has taken
preparatory steps, although there may be a veiled reference or ambiguous
or inconclusive evidence pointing to that possibility—an allusion to a book
or movie that shows planning of a violent act or a vague general statement
seeking to convey that the threat is not empty: “I’m serious!” or “I really
mean this!”
High Level of Threat: A threat that appears to pose an imminent and serious dan-
ger to the safety of others.
The threat is direct, specific, and plausible.
The threat suggests that concrete steps have been taken toward carrying it
out—for example, statements indicating that the person who issued the
threat has acquired or practiced with a weapon or has had the victim under
surveillance. (pp. 8–9)
The process of threat assessment must be conducted in an environment that will
calm the client, facilitate the de-escalation of intense emotions, and assist in the attain-
ment of precise information regarding the level of threat intent. The assessment of vio-
lence is like the assessment of other symptoms, in that the client’s history of violence,
family and medical history, mental status, and drug use must all be investigated. Van
Brunt (2015) underscored the need for a calm demeanor and the use of basic rapport-
building and active listening skills. Using counseling skills, a relationship can be formed,
and the clients will be more willing and able to open up about their thoughts and poten-
tial plans. Law enforcement representatives assert that the active listening skills that
counselors learn in their interpersonal listening classes are very similar to the rapport-
building strategies that crisis response or SWAT team members use in police or FBI work
(R. Jackson, personal communication, April 15, 2015; C. Spears, personal communication,
February 10, 2022).
Once rapport is established, the counselor can delve into precise questions to
obtain detailed information about the intent of the client to harm another, the extent

-- 231 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	203
and intrusiveness of the homicidal thoughts, and the ability of the client to acquire the
means to implement homicide plans. When dealing with a client expressing homicidal
intent, crisis counselors must always keep the ethical obligation of public safety and the
duty to warn at the forefront of their minds, even with low-threat clients. Furthermore,
while assessing homicidal ideation, the counselor should also assess for suicidal ideation
because homicide and suicide may co-occur. Now see Case Study 6.3, which presents a
situation where a counselor must assess a client’s level of threat. Then read Voices from
the Field 6.3 and complete Activity 6.3 on threat assessment.
CASE STUDY 6.3
Assessing for Level of Threat
Riley: 	I hate my supervisor at work. My life would be better if he was
dead.
Counselor: 	You’re so angry at your supervisor that you wish he was dead.
Have you had thoughts about harming him?
Riley: 	Sure, I think about killing him.
Counselor: 	Have you come up with a plan to kill him?
Riley: 	A plan? I mean I’ve got a 45 at home but no, not a plan exactly.
Counselor: 	Are there times when you feel you might act on these thoughts?
Riley: 	Ha, whenever I’m drunk.
Discussion Questions
1. What level of threat is indicated so far?
2. What other information does the counselor need to assess the threat level?
3. If the counselor determines the supervisor is in danger, what steps should the
counselor take?
